metadata.title: Disco: A Compact Index for LSM-trees
metadata.titleTranslation: Disco：一种面向 LSM-tree 的紧凑索引

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown: Many key-value stores and database systems use log-structured merge-trees (LSM-trees) as their storage engines because of their excellent write performance. However, the read performance of LSM-trees is suboptimal due to the overlapping sorted runs. Most existing efforts rely on filters to reduce unnecessary I/Os, but filters fundamentally do not help locate items and often become the bottleneck of the system. We identify that the lack of efficient index is the root cause of subpar read performance in LSM-trees. In this paper, we propose Disco: a compact index for LSM-trees. Disco indexes all the keys in an LSM-tree, so a query does not have to search every run of the LSM-tree. It records compact key representations to minimize the number of key comparisons so as to minimize cache misses and I/Os for both point and range queries. Disco guarantees that both point queries and seeks issue at most one I/O to the underlying runs, achieving an I/O efficiency close to a B$^+$-tree. Disco improves upon REMIX’s pioneering multi-run index design with additional compact key representations to help improve read performance. The representations are compact so the cost of persisting Disco to disk is small. Moreover, while a traditional LSM-tree has to choose a more aggressive compaction policy that slows down write performance to have better read performance, a Disco-indexed LSM-tree can employ a write-efficient policy and still have good read performance. Experimental results show that Disco can save I/Os and improve point and range query performance by up to 220% over RocksDB while maintaining efficient writes.
sections.translationMarkdown: 许多键值存储和数据库系统都使用日志结构合并树（log-structured merge-tree，LSM-tree）作为存储引擎，因为它具有出色的写入性能。然而，由于 sorted run 相互重叠，LSM-tree 的读取性能并不理想。现有工作大多依赖过滤器来减少不必要的 I/O，但过滤器从根本上说并不能帮助定位数据项，而且经常成为系统瓶颈。我们发现，缺少高效索引是 LSM-tree 读取性能欠佳的根本原因。本文提出 Disco：一种面向 LSM-tree 的紧凑索引。Disco 为 LSM-tree 中的所有键建立索引，因此查询不必搜索 LSM-tree 的每个 run。它记录紧凑的键表示，以尽量减少键比较次数，进而减少点查询和范围查询中的 cache miss 与 I/O。Disco 保证点查询和 seek 对底层 run 最多发出一次 I/O，从而实现接近 B$^+$-tree 的 I/O 效率。Disco 在 REMIX 开创性的 multi-run index 设计基础上进行了改进，加入额外的紧凑键表示来提升读取性能。这些表示非常紧凑，因此将 Disco 持久化到磁盘的开销很小。此外，传统 LSM-tree 为获得更好的读取性能，必须选择更激进、但会降低写入性能的 compaction 策略；而采用 Disco 索引的 LSM-tree 可以使用写入高效的策略，同时仍保持良好的读取性能。实验结果表明，Disco 能够节省 I/O，并且在保持高效写入的同时，相比 RocksDB 将点查询和范围查询性能最高提升 220%。

sections.title: 1 INTRODUCTION
sections.originalMarkdown:
The log-structured merge-trees (LSM-trees) [40] have been adopted in many database systems [21, 25, 33, 44] as their storage engines because it has better write and relatively acceptable read performance compared to traditional data structures such as B$^+$-trees. Unlike B$^+$-trees that perform expensive in-place writes on updates, LSM-trees enable large sequential disk writes by creating a new run of data on disk for every batch of updates. However, this out-of-place update approach has a negative impact on the read efficiency because a query has to check multiple runs to locate the requested data. To maintain a desirable read performance, LSM-trees use a compaction process to merge and rewrite existing runs, thereby reducing the number of runs that need to be accessed for a query. The compaction process trades increased background writes for reduced reads in future queries. Although there exist different compaction policies [16, 17, 43] that make different trade-offs between read and write costs, queries on an LSM-tree are still much more expensive than on a B$^+$-tree. It remains challenging to design an LSM-tree structure that achieves read performance comparable to a B$^+$-tree without sacrificing write efficiency.

Memory-resident Bloom filters [7] are one commonly adopted mechanism in LSM-trees to improve read performance at a negligible write cost. Using Bloom filters allows for identifying and skipping runs that do not contain the key of a search. However, Bloom filters may not be effective in common scenarios because of two major limitations. To begin with, Bloom filters can have a negative impact on query performance with a low memory budget [18, 38, 54]. When a Bloom filter is not memory-resident, loading it from disk causes extra I/Os and caching, which can be even worse than not using Bloom filters at all. Additionally, Bloom filters cannot handle range queries. Although range filters [36, 47, 48, 49, 51] are designed to identify and skip runs that do not contain a range of keys of a query, they are still not used in production systems for two main reasons. Firstly, the state-of-the-art range filters consume as much, if not more, memory than Bloom filters [36, 47, 48, 49, 51], making it inefficient when the memory is under pressure. Secondly, the interface of range filters does not support the iterator semantics widely used in current LSM-tree implementations for traversing key-value pairs incrementally.

Bloom and range filters offer limited benefits as they only reduce the number of runs that must be accessed, without helping locate data in a run directly. After filtering, LSM-trees still need to read additional blocks from disk to locate and retrieve the required data. The inefficiency of reads in LSM-trees is caused by the lack of indexes among the runs of data. Because each sorted run in an LSM-tree is an independent structure, a query has to read the metadata including indexes and filters on each run individually before the requested data can be located. REMIX [53] has explored the idea of multi-run indexes on LSM-trees. In REMIX, sorted runs are sparsely indexed by a B$^+$-tree-like structure, so queries can efficiently locate the required data without the need to search independently on each run. However, when the system has limited caching resources, read operations still incur superfluous read I/Os [48] as a query needs to access as many as $\log(n)$ sorted runs [53], where $n$ is the total number of sorted runs, to locate an item.

As the data size in LSM-tree systems grows, existing solutions struggle to keep a desirable read performance. This calls for a data structure that works well under memory pressure, maintains low read I/O cost similar to B$^+$-trees, and provides efficient write performance for LSM-trees.

We present Disco, a compact multi-run index that achieves efficient reads on LSM-trees. With Disco, point queries and range queries deterministically incur at most one read I/O to the underlying runs to locate the data on disk. Based on this, we build a Disco-indexed LSM-tree named DiscoDB that employs a write-efficient compaction policy, without sacrificing read performance. Experimental results show that Disco performs similarly to B$^+$-trees for read operations in terms of I/Os per query. DiscoDB achieves a significant read performance improvement while maintaining an efficient write performance compare to other LSM-tree designs.

This paper makes the following contributions. First, we identify the problem of inefficient indexes or the lack thereof in LSM-trees. Second, we propose Disco, an efficient index design for LSM-trees, that combines the compact key representations with REMIX’s multi-run index. Finally, we build and evaluate Disco as well as DiscoDB. Experimental results show that Disco can efficient index items on LSM-trees so that a point and range query use minimal I/Os when the cache budget is limited. We hope this work can fill the missing piece in the research of LSM-tree indexes.
sections.translationMarkdown:
日志结构合并树（log-structured merge-tree，LSM-tree）[40] 已被许多数据库系统 [21, 25, 33, 44] 用作存储引擎，因为与 B$^+$-tree 等传统数据结构相比，它具有更好的写入性能和相对可接受的读取性能。B$^+$-tree 在更新时会执行昂贵的原地写入；与之不同，LSM-tree 每积累一批更新，就在磁盘上创建一个新的数据 run，从而实现大规模顺序磁盘写入。然而，这种非原地更新方式会降低读取效率，因为查询必须检查多个 run 才能定位所请求的数据。为了保持理想的读取性能，LSM-tree 使用 compaction 过程合并并重写已有 run，从而减少查询需要访问的 run 数量。compaction 以增加后台写入为代价，减少未来查询的读取量。尽管存在不同的 compaction 策略 [16, 17, 43]，它们在读写成本之间作出不同权衡，但 LSM-tree 上的查询仍比 B$^+$-tree 上昂贵得多。如何在不牺牲写入效率的前提下，设计一种读取性能可与 B$^+$-tree 相媲美的 LSM-tree 结构，依然是一个挑战。

内存驻留的 Bloom filter [7] 是 LSM-tree 中一种常用机制，可以在写入开销几乎可忽略的情况下提升读取性能。借助 Bloom filter，可以识别并跳过不包含待搜索键的 run。然而，由于两个主要限制，Bloom filter 在常见场景中可能并不有效。首先，在内存预算较低时，Bloom filter 可能对查询性能产生负面影响 [18, 38, 54]。当 Bloom filter 不能驻留内存时，从磁盘加载它会产生额外的 I/O 和缓存开销，甚至可能比完全不使用 Bloom filter 更糟。此外，Bloom filter 无法处理范围查询。虽然 range filter [36, 47, 48, 49, 51] 旨在识别并跳过不包含查询键范围的 run，但它们仍未在生产系统中使用，主要有两个原因。第一，最先进的 range filter 所消耗的内存与 Bloom filter 一样多，甚至更多 [36, 47, 48, 49, 51]，因此在内存紧张时效率很低。第二，range filter 的接口不支持当前 LSM-tree 实现中广泛使用的 iterator 语义，而这种语义用于逐步遍历键值对。

Bloom filter 和 range filter 的收益有限，因为它们只能减少必须访问的 run 数量，却不能直接帮助在 run 内定位数据。过滤之后，LSM-tree 仍需从磁盘读取额外的数据块，才能定位并取回所需数据。LSM-tree 读取效率低下，是由各数据 run 之间缺少索引造成的。由于 LSM-tree 中每个 sorted run 都是独立结构，查询必须分别读取每个 run 上的 metadata（包括索引和过滤器），之后才能定位所请求的数据。REMIX [53] 探索了在 LSM-tree 上构建 multi-run index 的思路。在 REMIX 中，sorted run 由一种类似 B$^+$-tree 的结构进行稀疏索引，因此查询无需独立搜索每个 run，就能高效定位所需数据。然而，当系统的缓存资源有限时，读取操作仍会产生多余的读取 I/O [48]：为了定位一个数据项，查询最多需要访问 $\log(n)$ 个 sorted run [53]，其中 $n$ 是 sorted run 的总数。

随着 LSM-tree 系统中的数据规模增长，现有方案越来越难以维持理想的读取性能。因此，需要一种能够在内存压力下良好工作、保持与 B$^+$-tree 相近的低读取 I/O 成本，同时为 LSM-tree 提供高效写入性能的数据结构。

我们提出 Disco，一种能够在 LSM-tree 上实现高效读取的紧凑 multi-run index。使用 Disco 时，点查询和范围查询为定位磁盘上的数据，确定性地最多只会对底层 run 产生一次读取 I/O。在此基础上，我们构建了名为 DiscoDB、采用 Disco 索引的 LSM-tree。DiscoDB 使用写入高效的 compaction 策略，同时不牺牲读取性能。实验结果表明，按每次查询的 I/O 数量衡量，Disco 的读取操作表现与 B$^+$-tree 相近。与其他 LSM-tree 设计相比，DiscoDB 在保持高效写入性能的同时，显著提升了读取性能。

本文作出以下贡献。第一，我们指出了 LSM-tree 中索引低效或缺失的问题。第二，我们提出了面向 LSM-tree 的高效索引设计 Disco，它将紧凑键表示与 REMIX 的 multi-run index 相结合。最后，我们构建并评估了 Disco 和 DiscoDB。实验结果表明，Disco 可以高效地为 LSM-tree 中的数据项建立索引，使点查询和范围查询在缓存预算有限时使用最少的 I/O。我们希望本工作能够补上 LSM-tree 索引研究中缺失的一环。

sections.title: 2 BACKGROUND AND MOTIVATION
sections.originalMarkdown:
Over time, the ratio of main memory to permanent storage in computer systems has been shrinking because the price drop in DRAM has always been slower than that of hard drives and SSDs [15, 37, 38, 54]. As a result, many LSM-tree deployments use a modestly-sized main memory paired with much larger hard drives or SSDs to serve more data at a reasonable hardware cost. At the time of writing, a 32 GB DRAM stick costs about the same as an 4 TB HDD or a 2 TB SSD [15, 37], resulting in a memory-to-disk ratio of 1:128 or 1:64, respectively. Thus, it is increasingly important to get maximum utility out of relatively precious main memory resources.

Bloom filters are designed to improve read using a small memory footprint, but it is facing growing difficulties to scale up with the increasing disk-to-memory ratios. The assumption that Bloom filters can be cached in main memory no longer holds. To show how expensive accessing multiple runs in LSM-trees can become, we conduct an experiment to compare the I/O costs of an LSM-tree and a B$^+$-tree under different caching budgets.[^1] Figure 1 shows the average I/Os per query with varying memory budgets. As a read optimized index structure, the B$^+$-tree uses at most two I/Os to finish a query across all memory budgets. The LSM-tree has a point query cost similar to the B$^+$-tree only when the cache budget is more than 2% of the dataset size, which are sufficient to cache Bloom filters. However, when the memory budget is limited, it requires 6× more I/Os than the B$^+$-tree due to the cache misses when accessing Bloom filters. Even with Bloom filters, the point queries have about 80% of I/O cost of range queries, which directly search the requested key on each run. In conclusion, Bloom filters can incur superfluous I/Os and even degrade the read performance when the caching resource is scarce.

> **Fig. 1.** Numbers of I/Os per query for LSM-trees and B$^+$-trees with different caching budgets. The LSM-tree has 8 sorted runs, each is equipped with Bloom filters that will be effective only in point queries (probe).

Despite being useful for point queries when cached in memory, Bloom filters cannot handle range queries, which are an important workload in database systems. As one of the major workloads, range queries are included in the ubiquitous Yahoo Cloud Serving Benchmark (YCSB) [13]. Many relational database systems [32, 45] are built on top of LSM-trees. They issue range queries to the underlying LSM-trees for every SQL query [39]. Hence, range queries have been used as one of the key performance indicators in many systems [24, 36, 46, 51, 53]. Achieving efficient range queries is always one of the desiderata in many systems.

It is challenging to improve range query performance on LSM-trees. Many systems implement range queries using iterators that support seek and next operations. A range query first performs a seek operation to find the start key of the query. Then, a number of next operations could be used to retrieve the subsequent keys until a certain condition is met. Oftentimes, the user-provided search key does not exist in the LSM-tree. In that case, a seek operation needs to find the smallest key that is greater than the search key. Because the sorted runs overlap in ranges, an LSM-tree needs to perform a seek operation on every run to find the required data, slowing down range queries.

Range filters have been proposed to try to solve the problem similarly to Bloom filters. However, range filters do not support the iterator semantics. They handle generalized range queries in the form of: “Is there a key in $[l, h]$ in the set $S$?” [51]. To apply range filters in LSM-trees, users must input a predetermined query range, which may not be known in practical scenarios. For example, in YCSB workload E, a range query consists of a seek and 50 next operations. Because a seek operation corresponds to an open range query, range filters can help only when the search key is greater than the largest key in the run.

Although filters can rule out runs that do not contain the target key and save I/Os when the memory is sufficiently large to cache them, they may also harm query performance because they do not help locate the keys in a run. For example, a range filter is expected to return positive for long range queries because the range is likely to cover most sorted runs. When the filter returns true, LSM-trees have to load the index blocks [20, 26] from disk to locate the desired data. In that case, the range filter does not save I/Os but brings overhead to queries. The same problem exists when filtering existing keys using point filters. Had we known that the keys of interest do exist, we would not have consulted filters.

The limitations of the filtering approaches have motivated the research on auxiliary indexes on LSM-trees. In [53], the authors propose a multi-run index on LSM-trees named REMIX to help improve range query performance. It records the access path of the keys so that a range query does not need to sort-merge the keys on the fly. It is effective only when the system has plentiful caching budget or runs on fast storage devices such as 3D-XPoint (Intel’s Optane SSD) where the cost of sort-merging is more prominent. However, if the system runs on commodity hardware such as an SSD with a limited caching budget, the cost of any additional I/O quickly outstrips the sort-merge savings, so REMIX becomes ineffective.

In this paper, we introduce Disco, an auxiliary index on LSM-trees, that maintains the write efficiency of LSM-trees and achieves a similar read efficiency to B$^+$-trees even under scarce memory budgets.

[^1]: In the experiment, we issue queries in a uniformly random manner and record the I/O traces of a small partition in an LSM-tree or B$^+$-tree. The partitions have 256 MB of key-value pairs, consisting of 1.8 millions email address keys and 120-byte values. The I/O traces are later replayed on a cache simulator with different cache size ranging from 0.1% to 100% of the dataset size to measure the number of I/Os per query. The LSM-tree has eight overlapping sorted runs each of which is 32 MB, matching the size of SSTable in popular implementation of LSM-trees [21, 22]. Each run is equipped with a Bloom filter that is configured to use a typical setup of 10 bits per key [22] to maintain a false positive rate about 1%. A point query on the LSM-tree first checks the Bloom filters, while a range query searches on each run and sort-merges the results. The B$^+$-tree stores all the data in sorted order in one file, and it does not use Bloom filters so the point and range queries in B$^+$-tree share exactly the same code path.
sections.translationMarkdown:
随着时间推移，计算机系统中主存与永久存储的比例一直在下降，因为 DRAM 的降价速度始终慢于硬盘和 SSD [15, 37, 38, 54]。因此，许多 LSM-tree 部署采用容量适中的主存，搭配容量大得多的硬盘或 SSD，以合理的硬件成本服务更多数据。在本文撰写时，一条 32 GB DRAM 的价格与一块 4 TB HDD 或一块 2 TB SSD 大致相同 [15, 37]，对应的内存与磁盘容量比分别为 1:128 和 1:64。因此，如何最大限度地利用相对宝贵的主存资源变得愈发重要。

Bloom filter 旨在用较小的内存占用改善读取，但随着磁盘与内存比例不断增大，它在扩展方面面临越来越多的困难。Bloom filter 可以缓存在主存中的假设已不再成立。为了展示访问 LSM-tree 中多个 run 的代价可能有多高，我们开展实验，在不同缓存预算下比较 LSM-tree 与 B$^+$-tree 的 I/O 成本。[^1] 图 1 展示了不同内存预算下每次查询的平均 I/O 数。作为一种读取优化的索引结构，B$^+$-tree 在所有内存预算下最多使用两次 I/O 即可完成查询。只有当缓存预算超过数据集大小的 2%、足以缓存 Bloom filter 时，LSM-tree 的点查询成本才与 B$^+$-tree 相近。然而，当内存预算有限时，由于访问 Bloom filter 时发生 cache miss，它所需的 I/O 比 B$^+$-tree 多 6 倍。即使使用 Bloom filter，点查询的 I/O 成本也约为范围查询的 80%；范围查询会直接在每个 run 上搜索所请求的键。总之，在缓存资源稀缺时，Bloom filter 可能产生多余 I/O，甚至降低读取性能。

> **图 1.** 不同缓存预算下，LSM-tree 和 B$^+$-tree 每次查询的 I/O 数量。该 LSM-tree 有 8 个 sorted run，每个都配备 Bloom filter；Bloom filter 只对点查询（probe）有效。

Bloom filter 驻留内存时虽对点查询有用，却无法处理范围查询，而范围查询是数据库系统中的一种重要负载。范围查询作为主要负载之一，被纳入广泛使用的 Yahoo Cloud Serving Benchmark（YCSB）[13]。许多关系数据库系统 [32, 45] 构建在 LSM-tree 之上，它们对每条 SQL 查询都会向底层 LSM-tree 发出范围查询 [39]。因此，范围查询已被许多系统用作关键性能指标之一 [24, 36, 46, 51, 53]。实现高效范围查询始终是许多系统追求的目标之一。

提升 LSM-tree 上的范围查询性能很有挑战性。许多系统使用支持 seek 和 next 操作的 iterator 来实现范围查询。范围查询首先执行 seek 操作，查找查询的起始键；随后可以执行若干次 next 操作，取回后续键，直至满足某个条件。用户提供的搜索键往往并不存在于 LSM-tree 中。在这种情况下，seek 操作需要找到大于搜索键的最小键。由于 sorted run 的键范围相互重叠，LSM-tree 必须在每个 run 上执行 seek 才能找到所需数据，从而拖慢范围查询。

研究者提出了 range filter，试图以类似 Bloom filter 的方式解决该问题。然而，range filter 不支持 iterator 语义。它所处理的是如下形式的广义范围查询：“集合 $S$ 中是否存在位于 $[l, h]$ 内的键？”[51]。要在 LSM-tree 中应用 range filter，用户必须输入预先确定的查询范围，而实际场景中可能无法预先知道这个范围。例如，在 YCSB workload E 中，一次范围查询由一次 seek 和 50 次 next 操作组成。由于 seek 操作对应开放式范围查询，只有当搜索键大于 run 中的最大键时，range filter 才能提供帮助。

当内存足以缓存过滤器时，过滤器可以排除不含目标键的 run 并节省 I/O；但它们也可能损害查询性能，因为它们无法帮助定位 run 内的键。例如，对于长范围查询，range filter 预计会返回 positive，因为查询范围很可能覆盖大多数 sorted run。过滤器返回 true 后，LSM-tree 必须从磁盘加载 index block [20, 26] 来定位所需数据。在这种情况下，range filter 并未节省 I/O，反而为查询带来了开销。使用 point filter 过滤实际存在的键时也有同样的问题。如果我们事先知道感兴趣的键确实存在，就根本不会查询过滤器。

过滤方法的局限性推动了 LSM-tree 辅助索引的研究。在文献 [53] 中，作者提出了一种名为 REMIX 的 LSM-tree multi-run index，以帮助改善范围查询性能。它记录键的访问路径，使范围查询无需即时对键进行 sort-merge。只有当系统拥有充足缓存预算，或运行在 3D-XPoint（Intel Optane SSD）等高速存储设备上、因而 sort-merge 成本更加突出时，该方法才有效。然而，如果系统运行在缓存预算有限的 SSD 等普通硬件上，任何额外 I/O 的成本都会迅速超过 sort-merge 带来的节省，因此 REMIX 将失去效果。

本文介绍 Disco，一种用于 LSM-tree 的辅助索引。它既保持 LSM-tree 的写入效率，又能在内存预算稀缺时实现与 B$^+$-tree 相近的读取效率。

[^1]: 在实验中，我们以均匀随机方式发出查询，并记录 LSM-tree 或 B$^+$-tree 中一个小分区的 I/O trace。该分区包含 256 MB 键值对，由 180 万个电子邮件地址键和 120 字节的值构成。之后，我们在 cache simulator 上重放 I/O trace，并将缓存大小设为数据集大小的 0.1% 至 100%，以测量每次查询的 I/O 数。LSM-tree 有 8 个相互重叠的 sorted run，每个大小为 32 MB，与流行 LSM-tree 实现中的 SSTable 大小一致 [21, 22]。每个 run 都配有 Bloom filter，采用每个键 10 bit 的典型配置 [22]，以维持约 1% 的 false positive rate。LSM-tree 上的点查询首先检查 Bloom filter，而范围查询会搜索每个 run，并对结果执行 sort-merge。B$^+$-tree 将所有数据按序存储在一个文件中，而且不使用 Bloom filter，因此 B$^+$-tree 中的点查询和范围查询采用完全相同的代码路径。

sections.title: 3 DESIGN
sections.originalMarkdown:
> **Fig. 2.** An overview of Disco. Each square box represents a segment in Disco.

> **Fig. 3.** An example segment in Disco. The segment contains of 5 keys: 0100 01, 0100 1, 0111, 0111 0, and 0111 10. The arrows show the ordering of the keys.

At a high level, Disco is an index across all runs of an LSM-tree. As shown in Figure 2, Disco divides the ordered set of all key-value pairs contained in the tree into short segments indexed by a search tree. Here, each segment indexes a bounded number of consecutive keys. The use of segments effectively reduces the height of the search tree, ensuring that the tree has a small memory footprint.

In Section 3.1, We describe how the search tree in Disco efficiently locates a target segment that indexes the target key and explain how the metadata in a segment is used to allow for accessing any keys in the segment efficiently. The metadata of segments themselves are small contiguous data structures that may be kept in memory, or read from disk using a single I/O. In Section 3.2, we propose a novel, compact representation of a segment. The compact representation enables Disco to identify the position of a target key using at most one I/O to the runs of the LSM-tree. As a result, Disco is able to return the target key within any one of the runs, using 1–2 I/O operations. Section 3.3 explains how Disco is efficiently maintained when the LSM-tree undergoes compactions. While Disco speeds up merging in compactions, a compaction can also provide hints to help update Disco.
sections.translationMarkdown:
> **图 2.** Disco 概览。每个方框代表 Disco 中的一个 segment。

> **图 3.** Disco 中的一个示例 segment。该 segment 包含 5 个键：0100 01、0100 1、0111、0111 0 和 0111 10。箭头表示这些键的顺序。

从整体上看，Disco 是一个跨越 LSM-tree 所有 run 的索引。如图 2 所示，Disco 将树中所有键值对的有序集合划分为由 search tree 索引的短 segment。每个 segment 为数量有上限的一组连续键建立索引。使用 segment 可以有效降低 search tree 的高度，确保该树仅占用较小的内存空间。

在第 3.1 节中，我们说明 Disco 的 search tree 如何高效定位为目标键建立索引的目标 segment，并解释如何利用 segment 中的 metadata 高效访问该 segment 内的任意键。segment metadata 本身是较小的连续数据结构，可以保存在内存中，也可以通过单次 I/O 从磁盘读取。在第 3.2 节中，我们提出一种新颖、紧凑的 segment 表示。借助这种紧凑表示，Disco 最多只需对 LSM-tree 的 run 执行一次 I/O，就能确定目标键的位置。因此，Disco 可以使用 1–2 次 I/O，从任意一个 run 中返回目标键。第 3.3 节解释了 LSM-tree 执行 compaction 时如何高效维护 Disco。Disco 可以加速 compaction 中的合并，而 compaction 也可以提供提示来帮助更新 Disco。

sections.title: 3.1 The Search Tree and Segments
sections.originalMarkdown:
The keys in a Disco-indexed LSM-tree are divided into variable length segments. Each segment has an anchor key that represents the lower bound of the segment and also the upper bound (exclusive) of the previous segment if it exists. The segments are indexed by a B$^+$-tree by their anchor keys, while the values encode each segment’s metadata. Thus, a query finds the target segment by finding the largest key that is less than or equal to the user input key.

Figure 3 shows an example of a segment in the search tree covering a subset of data in three runs. In this example, we use the smallest key in a segment as the anchor key. We can reduce storage cost further by using only the minimal unique prefix of this key as the segment’s anchor key.

The metadata of a segment contains a set of cursor offsets, a set of run selectors, and a compact segment representation that consists of a partial key mask, and partial keys. The cursor offsets and run selectors are borrowed from REMIX [53] and they serve similar purposes. The usage of the partial key mask and partial keys will be discussed later in Section 3.2. Cursor offsets record the positions of the smallest key in each run that belongs to the segment. In our example, $p_0$, $p_1$, and $p_2$ point to the string keys 0100 1, 0111 10, and 0100 01, respectively. The iterators of each run will be initialized to point to those keys. Run selectors correspond to the keys in a segment. It records the access order of the runs when scanning the segment. In this example, the run selectors 2, 0, 2, 0, 1 indicate that the sorted view of this segment can be generated by reading the keys pointed to by the iterators of the runs in the order of $R_2, R_0, R_2, R_0, R_1$. After we finish reading a key, the iterator will move forward to track the next key on the run.

Segment metadata enables Disco to efficiently access any keys in the segment using minimal I/Os. We can access a key at any position in the segment by counting the number of occurrences of run selectors. In this example, if we want to access the third key 0111 in the segment we can first look at the third run selector, 2, the one corresponding to the same position. By looking at the run selectors preceding the third run selector in this segment, it is trivial to find that this is the second occurrence in the segment. The third key could be retrieved by reading the second key in $R_2$ after we initialize the iterator of the run with cursor offset $p_2$. With the help of the segment metadata, we can randomly access any key in a segment by its index using only one I/O.
sections.translationMarkdown:
采用 Disco 索引的 LSM-tree 中，键被划分为长度可变的 segment。每个 segment 都有一个 anchor key，它表示该 segment 的下界；如果存在前一个 segment，它同时也是前一 segment 的上界（不包含该界值）。B$^+$-tree 根据 anchor key 对各 segment 建立索引，而 value 则编码每个 segment 的 metadata。因此，查询通过查找小于或等于用户输入键的最大键来找到目标 segment。

图 3 展示了 search tree 中一个 segment 的示例，该 segment 覆盖三个 run 中的一部分数据。在这个例子中，我们使用 segment 中的最小键作为 anchor key。也可以只使用该键的最短唯一前缀作为 segment 的 anchor key，以进一步降低存储成本。

segment 的 metadata 包含一组 cursor offset、一组 run selector，以及由 partial key mask 和 partial key 构成的紧凑 segment 表示。cursor offset 和 run selector 借鉴自 REMIX [53]，用途也与之类似。partial key mask 和 partial key 的用法将在第 3.2 节讨论。cursor offset 记录每个 run 中属于该 segment 的最小键的位置。在我们的例子中，$p_0$、$p_1$ 和 $p_2$ 分别指向字符串键 0100 1、0111 10 和 0100 01。每个 run 的 iterator 都会初始化为指向这些键。run selector 与 segment 中的键一一对应，记录扫描 segment 时访问各 run 的顺序。在这个例子中，run selector 2、0、2、0、1 表明，可以依次读取 run iterator 所指向的键，即按 $R_2, R_0, R_2, R_0, R_1$ 的顺序，生成该 segment 的有序视图。每读取完一个键，iterator 都会向前移动，跟踪该 run 上的下一个键。

segment metadata 使 Disco 能够使用最少的 I/O，高效访问 segment 中的任意键。通过统计 run selector 的出现次数，可以访问 segment 中任意位置的键。在这个例子中，如果要访问 segment 中的第三个键 0111，可以先查看同一位置对应的第三个 run selector，即 2。查看该 segment 中位于第三个 run selector 之前的 run selector，可以直接发现这是 2 在 segment 中的第二次出现。使用 cursor offset $p_2$ 初始化该 run 的 iterator 后，读取 $R_2$ 中的第二个键即可取回第三个键。借助 segment metadata，我们可以按索引随机访问 segment 中的任意键，并且只需一次 I/O。

sections.title: 3.2 The Compact Segment Representation
sections.originalMarkdown: Central to the compact segment representation is the idea of discriminative bits. Below, we first provide an intuition, using string keys and discriminative characters for illustrative purposes. We then describe the actual design in detail, which is based on individual discriminative bits, rather than characters.
sections.translationMarkdown: 紧凑 segment 表示的核心思想是 discriminative bit。下面，我们首先使用字符串键和 discriminative character 进行说明，以给出直观理解；随后详细介绍实际设计。实际设计基于单个 discriminative bit，而非字符。

sections.title: 3.2.1 Intuition: discriminative characters.
sections.originalMarkdown:
The discriminative characters of a segment of keys are the characters that make a difference to the ordering of the keys within the segment. For example, if a segment contains three string keys “disconnected”, “discontinuous”, and “discrepancy”, only the character after “disc” and the seventh character, both bold-faced, are discriminative. The other characters can be elided, without altering the internal order.

Conceptually, it is sufficient to represent only the discriminative characters in the index, as opposed to the entire keys, which helps keep the index compact. In our running example above, we may use the string “disc” as the anchor key for our segment. We may then represent the individual keys using only the fifth and seventh characters “on”, “ot” and “rp”.

Given a query for a key that falls within the segment key range, say “discount”, we can extract the discriminative characters from the key, and quickly find its position within the sorted index. In this case, the compact search key “on” would match with the compact representation of “disconnected”, yielding the on-disk location where the key “discount” may (or may not, in this example) be found.

The search key “discovery” presents a problem, however. Here, the ‘v’ is in fact discriminative for the search key, but not represented in the segment. After extracting the two discriminative characters of the segment, which were selected without knowledge of the search key, the compact representation of “discovery” is “oe”. Using “oe” to search the compact key would lead us to the location of “disconnected”, namely “on” in compact keys, which is not the segment key result that should have been found using the original search key.

Should we need to find the correct segment key, we can achieve this goal with a single additional search, after a small update to the compact search key. First, we compare the full search key “discovery” and the result key “disconnected” and find their shared prefix: “disco”. Intuitively, the key we are looking for is the largest key following the shared prefix, because of the ‘v’ in “discovery” being greater than the ‘n’ in “disconnected”. To find this key, we may simply set all the discriminative characters in the search key, after this shared prefix, to the maximum value (say, ‘z’). Thus, we search the index a second time, but this time for “oz” instead of “on”, which finally yields the search result “discrepancy”, the correct result key in the segment.

As noted, Disco does not use discriminative characters, but rather discriminative bits in key strings. A detailed description follows below.
sections.translationMarkdown:
一个键 segment 的 discriminative character，是会影响该 segment 内键的排列顺序的字符。例如，如果一个 segment 包含三个字符串键 “disconnected”“discontinuous” 和 “discrepancy”，那么只有 “disc” 后面的字符和第七个字符（原文均以粗体标出）是 discriminative character。省略其他字符并不会改变这些键的内部顺序。

从概念上说，索引中只需表示 discriminative character，而不必表示完整键，这有助于保持索引紧凑。在上述贯穿示例中，可以使用字符串 “disc” 作为 segment 的 anchor key。随后，只用第五和第七个字符 “on”“ot” 和 “rp” 来分别表示各个键。

给定一个落在该 segment 键范围内的查询键，例如 “discount”，我们可以从该键中提取 discriminative character，并迅速找到它在有序索引中的位置。在这种情况下，紧凑搜索键 “on” 会匹配 “disconnected” 的紧凑表示，从而得到磁盘上的位置；键 “discount” 可能位于该位置，也可能像本例一样不存在。

然而，搜索键 “discovery” 会带来一个问题。此时，‘v’ 对搜索键而言实际上具有区分作用，但它没有在 segment 中得到表示。从 “discovery” 中提取 segment 的两个 discriminative character 后，会得到紧凑表示 “oe”；而 segment 中这两个 discriminative character 是在不知道搜索键的情况下选出的。使用 “oe” 搜索紧凑键，会将我们引向 “disconnected” 的位置，即紧凑键中的 “on”，但这不是使用原始搜索键本应找到的 segment 键结果。

如果需要找到正确的 segment 键，只需略微更新紧凑搜索键，再额外搜索一次即可。首先，我们比较完整搜索键 “discovery” 与结果键 “disconnected”，找到二者的共同前缀 “disco”。直观上，由于 “discovery” 中的 ‘v’ 大于 “disconnected” 中的 ‘n’，我们要找的是跟在这一共同前缀之后的最大键。为了找到这个键，只需将搜索键中位于共同前缀之后的所有 discriminative character 设为最大值（例如 ‘z’）。因此，我们对索引执行第二次搜索，但这次搜索 “oz” 而不是 “on”，最终得到搜索结果 “discrepancy”，即 segment 中正确的结果键。

如前所述，Disco 并不使用 discriminative character，而是使用键字符串中的 discriminative bit。下面给出详细说明。

sections.title: 3.2.2 Design details: discriminative bits.
sections.originalMarkdown:
The segment representation encodes a group of sorted variable-length string keys into an integer array, shown as partial keys in Figure 3, and a bit vector, partial key mask, where 1s mark the positions of discriminative bits. The partial key mask will be used to extract bits from a search key from user input and generate a partial key of the search key. The new partial key is then used for search on the sorted integer array.

*Construction.* The compact segment representation is constructed in two steps. The first step is to compute the partial key mask of a group of keys. Then we will use the partial key mask to extract the partial keys from them. The time complexity of this step is linear to the number of keys in the group. The pseudocode of this process is shown in Algorithm 1.

**Algorithm 1: The process of building a partial key mask and partial keys**

```text
1: function Build(string_keys)
2:    pkmask ← 000...0
3:    pkeys ← ∅
4:    for i ∈ [0, string_keys.len − 2] do
5:        lcp ← bitwise_lcp(string_keys[i], string_keys[i+1])
6:        mark_bit_at(pkmask, lcp)
7:    for i ∈ [0, string_keys.len − 1] do
8:        pkeys[i] ← bit_extract(string_keys[i], pkmask)
9:    return pkmask, pkeys
```

We derive the partial key mask by iterating through each pair of neighboring keys in sorted order. The first loop in Algorithm 1 shows the steps. The partial key mask is a bit vector that marks the position of discriminative bits. The discriminative bit of a pair of keys is defined as the first mismatching bit between the two. The location of the discriminative bit is captured by the length of the bitwise common prefix of the two strings. We set the corresponding position to one in the partial key mask to collect all the discriminative bit positions. For a group of $n$ keys, we need to go through all $n - 1$ pairs of neighboring keys. It follows that $n$ keys can produce $n - 1$ discriminative bits, out of which at most $n - 1$ are distinct. As a result, at most $n - 1$ bits can be set to 1 in the partial key mask.

A partial key is generated by extracting the bits from a string key at the positions where the partial key mask is set to one. The extraction uses parallel bit extract operations [11] to select non-contiguous bits from strings. The partial key mask is used as the bit mask for the parallel bit extract operations. For one string key, it outputs a partial key whose length equals to the total number of bits set to 1 in the partial key mask. Because the partial key mask marks where each pair of keys start to differ, the generated partial keys are unique. Since the original string keys are sorted and the partial key mask captures where the bit changes from 0 to 1, the partial keys are inherently sorted.

Figure 4 shows an example of generating a partial key mask and partial keys. We iterate through each pair of adjacent keys to find the discriminative bits. In this example, the first pair key$_1$ 0100 01 and key$_2$ 0100 1 differ at the fifth bit indicated by the dashed box, so the discriminative bit is at bit position 4 in zero-based index. The second pair key$_2$ 0100 1 and key$_3$ 0111 give us another discriminative bit at bit position 2. If one key is the prefix of another and the longer one ends at 0, such as the third pair key$_3$ 0111 and key$_4$ 0111 0 in this example, then there is not a discriminative bit for them. The final pair key$_4$ 0111 0 and key$_5$ 0111 10 happen to have the discriminative bit at bit position 4. In this case, three discriminative bits result in two bits set to one in the partial key mask 0010 1.

> **Fig. 4.** An example of generating partial key mask and partial keys for binary string keys: {0100 01, 0100 1, 0111, 0111 0, 0111 10}. These keys generate a partial key mask: 0010 10 and partial keys {00, 01, 10, 10, 11}.

To extract the partial keys, we iterate through the string keys and perform parallel bit extract operations. As shown in Figure 4, the partial keys selected from the bits at bit positions 2 and 4. When a bit position is out of bound for a key, such as the case for key$_3$, we will logically pad 0s to it. Because key$_3$ and key$_4$ do not generate a discriminative bit, their partial keys are the same. We treat them as the same key during queries.

*Point queries.* Performing point queries on the compact segment representation is very efficient. Similarly to a point filter, an empty point query often returns key-does-not-exist without using any I/Os to the LSM-tree, and it sometimes needs to issue I/Os to verify the existence of the key when it has false positives. The compact segment representation guarantees that only one I/O is needed in that case. Point queries of existing keys take at most one I/O to locate the key.

**Algorithm 2: The algorithm of point queries on a compact segment representation**

```text
1: function Point_Query(search_key, pkeys, pkmask)
2:    pkey ← bit_extract(search_key, pkmask)
3:    pos ← find_exact_match(pkeys, pkey)
4:    if pos = Not found then
5:        return Not found
6:    result_key ← string_key_at(pos)
7:    cmp ← str_cmp(probe_key, search_key)
8:    if cmp = 0 then
9:        return pos
10:   return Not found
```

Algorithm 2 shows the process of performing point queries. Given a search key, the first step is to extract the partial key of the search key using the partial key mask. We extract the partial key from the search key in the same way how partial keys are extracted. If the search key is shorter than the discriminative bit vector, we logically pad 0s at the end of the search key. That will give us a partial key of the same length as the stored partial keys.

We then search in the partial key array to find an entry that exactly matches the partial key extracted from the search key. If an exact match is not found, we can conclude that the search key does not exist without accessing any keys in the segment. Otherwise, there exists a partial key that matches the partial key of the search key. We then issue an I/O to access the string key at the corresponding position as shown at line 6 in Algorithm 2. We return the index of the result key only if it matches the search key.

*Range queries.* It takes additional steps to perform a range query on the compact segment representation. Similar to performing a point query, the first step of doing a range query is to extract a partial key of the search key from the user. The partial key is then used as the search key to perform a longest prefix match (LPM) in the array of partial keys. It returns the position of a partial key in the partial key array that best matches the prefix of the partial key extracted from the user. Similarly to point queries, we will issue an I/O to access the string key at the position and compare it to the search key. It directly returns the result position if the search key happens to match the accessed string key. Otherwise, it evaluates how many bits in the partial key mask that these two keys have matched. Analogous to the intuition in Section 3.2.1, we can derive an updated partial key based on the knowledge of the number of matched bits and the ordering between the accessed string key and the search key. Finally, the result position will be found by performing a regular search on the partial keys with the updated partial key of the search key. The detailed process of performing a query on the compact segment representation is shown in Algorithms 3 and 4.

**Algorithm 3: The algorithm of range queries on a compact segment representation**

```text
1: function Range_Query(search_key, pkeys, pkmask)
2:    pkey ← bit_extract(search_key, pkmask)
3:    pos ← longest_prefix_match(pkeys, pkey)
4:    probe_key ← string_key_at(pos)
5:    cmp ← str_cmp(probe_key, search_key)
6:    if cmp = 0 then
7:        return pos
8:    n_mismatch ← mismatch_count(probe_key, search_key, pkmask)
9:    if mismatch_count = 0 then
10:       if cmp < 0 then
11:           return pos
12:       else
13:           return pos + 1
14:   if cmp < 0 then
15:       new_pkey ← pkey_lowest(pkey, n_mismatch)
16:   else
17:       new_pkey ← pkey_highest(pkey, n_mismatch)
18:   pos ← pkey_search(pkeys, new_pkey)
19:   return pos
```

The following paragraphs discuss how we use the partial key mask and partial keys to locate a search key in detail. For the sake of simplicity, we define a seek operation as finding the smallest key that is greater than or equal to the search key. We assume each range query starts with a seek operation. To perform a range query, the first step is similar to point queries. What is different is that after extracting the partial key, we might not find a partial key that exactly matches the partial key of the search key. Should we indeed find a matching partial key, the string key at the result position might not match the search key: a case of “false positive”. In both cases, we still want to finish the range query using the least I/Os. To do that, searching in the partial keys needs to mimic the matching process on a radix tree (trie) where we find the LPM partial key.

When the position of the LPM partial key is determined, we will read the actual string key at the same position in the segment, denoted as the probe key in the following. This is the only place where we issue an I/O and do a string comparison. Having the actual string key, we can determine if it is an exact match. If it is, we return the position immediately. Otherwise, we learn that the probe key is either greater or smaller than the search key.

Having accessed the actual string key infers not just the ordering of the search key and the probe key, but also the number of matched characters or bits between the two keys. The length of the longest common prefix (LCP) exactly captures this metric. If we think of all the keys in a segment are organized in a trie, then each internal node represents a common prefix of all its leaves, and all the keys in the subtree rooted in the node share the common prefix of that node. A seek operation of the search key is equivalent to finding either the leftmost leaf or the next key after the rightmost leaf in the subtree. If the probe key is greater than the search tree, we need to find the leftmost leaf. Otherwise, the probe key is smaller than the search tree, and we look for the next key after the rightmost leaf.

Directly doing range queries on the real keys triggers excessive I/Os. Instead, we construct a new partial key that would return the same position when it is used to perform a search in the partial keys. To do that, the first step is to find the number of bits that the LCP matches the partial key mask. For easier of handling in the next step, we compute the number of mismatching bits in the implementation as shown in function mismatch_count in Algorithm 4. If the number of mismatching bits is zero, that implies that either the search key or the probe key is the prefix of the other. The function returns the current position or the next position depending on the key comparison result.

**Algorithm 4: Auxiliary functions of range queries**

```text
function mismatch_count(probe_key, search_key, pkmask)
   lcp_bit ← bitewise_lcp(probe_key, search_key)
   matched_bits ← pop_count(pkmask[: lcp_bit])
   tot_ones ← pop_count(pkmask)
   return tot_ones - matched_bits

function pkey_highest(pkey, mismatch_count)
   bit ← shift_left(1, mismatch_count)
   mask ← bit - 1
   highest ← bitwise_or(highest, mask)
   highest ← highest + 1
   return highest

function pkey_lowest(pkey, mismatch_count)
   bit ← shift_left(1, mismatch_count)
   mask ← reverse_bit(bit - 1)
   if pkey < bit then
       return 0
   lowest ← bitwise_and(pkey, mask)
   return lowest
```

In the second step, we will use the number of mismatching bits to construct a new partial key that could lead to the leftmost leaf or the key after the rightmost leaf of it. If the search key is greater than the probe key, we will construct a new partial key that fills all the trailing mismatching bits in the partial key of the search key with all 0s, as shown in function pkey_lowest. Otherwise, we fill them with all 1s then arithmetically plus one. The new partial key is then used to do a seek operation in the partial keys. The position we get from this search is guaranteed to be the position of a seek operation of search key in the segment.

> **Fig. 5.** An example of range query using Disco illustrated in a radix tree (trie).

*A range query example.* To better illustrate how range queries mimic the search on a trie, we construct a Patricia trie in Figure 5 using the example keys in Figure 4. With the search key 0101 10 and the partial key mask 0010 10, we get the partial key of the search key 01. Next, we try to find the LPM partial key, which is equivalent to following the Patricia trie to match the tokens. It goes to the partial key 01, which has the corresponding string key 0100 1. This key is denoted as the probe key in Algorithm 3. By comparing the probe key to the search key, we learn that the search key is greater than the probe key hence we need to find the key after the rightmost leave of the LPM subtree. We then call the auxiliary function mismatch_count in Algorithm 4 to find the number of mismatching bits in the partial keys. It shows that the probe key and the search key share 3 bits of common prefix, which translates to only one bit 0 matched in the partial key mask. Correspondingly, there is one mismatching bit in the partial key mask. Knowing the probe key is smaller than the search key, we construct a new partial key using the auxiliary function pkey_highest in Algorithm 4. It fills the trailing mismatching bit with a 1 and then adds one to it, resulting in a new partial key 10. When we use this new key to search in the array again, it returns the position of the third partial key in the array. This position is indeed the result position of the finding the search key 0101 10 in this group of string keys.
sections.translationMarkdown:
segment 表示将一组有序的变长字符串键编码为一个整数数组（即图 3 中的 partial key）和一个名为 partial key mask 的 bit vector，其中值为 1 的位置标记 discriminative bit。partial key mask 用于从用户输入的搜索键中提取 bit，并生成该搜索键的 partial key。随后，使用这个新的 partial key 在有序整数数组中进行搜索。

*构建。* 紧凑 segment 表示分两步构建。第一步计算一组键的 partial key mask；随后使用 partial key mask 从这些键中提取 partial key。该步骤的时间复杂度与组内键的数量呈线性关系。算法 1 给出了这一过程的伪代码。

**算法 1：构建 partial key mask 和 partial key 的过程**

```text
1: function Build(string_keys)
2:    pkmask ← 000...0
3:    pkeys ← ∅
4:    for i ∈ [0, string_keys.len − 2] do
5:        lcp ← bitwise_lcp(string_keys[i], string_keys[i+1])
6:        mark_bit_at(pkmask, lcp)
7:    for i ∈ [0, string_keys.len − 1] do
8:        pkeys[i] ← bit_extract(string_keys[i], pkmask)
9:    return pkmask, pkeys
```

我们按排序顺序遍历每一对相邻键，以推导 partial key mask。算法 1 的第一个循环展示了这些步骤。partial key mask 是一个标记 discriminative bit 位置的 bit vector。一对键的 discriminative bit 被定义为二者第一个不相同的 bit。两个字符串按位公共前缀的长度，恰好给出了 discriminative bit 的位置。我们将 partial key mask 中的相应位置设为 1，从而收集所有 discriminative bit 位置。对于一组 $n$ 个键，需要遍历全部 $n - 1$ 对相邻键。因此，$n$ 个键可以产生 $n - 1$ 个 discriminative bit，其中最多有 $n - 1$ 个互不相同。由此，partial key mask 中最多有 $n - 1$ 个 bit 被设为 1。

partial key 的生成方式是：从字符串键中提取 partial key mask 中被设为 1 的位置上的 bit。提取过程使用 parallel bit extract 操作 [11]，从字符串中选择不连续的 bit。partial key mask 被用作 parallel bit extract 操作的 bit mask。对于一个字符串键，该操作输出一个 partial key，其长度等于 partial key mask 中设为 1 的 bit 总数。由于 partial key mask 标记了每对键开始出现差异的位置，因此生成的 partial key 是唯一的。又因为原始字符串键已有序，而 partial key mask 捕获了 bit 从 0 变为 1 的位置，所以 partial key 天然有序。

图 4 展示了生成 partial key mask 和 partial key 的示例。我们遍历每一对相邻键来查找 discriminative bit。在该示例中，第一对键 key$_1$ 0100 01 与 key$_2$ 0100 1 在虚线框指示的第 5 个 bit 处不同，因此在从 0 开始的索引中，discriminative bit 位于 bit 位置 4。第二对键 key$_2$ 0100 1 和 key$_3$ 0111 在 bit 位置 2 处产生另一个 discriminative bit。如果一个键是另一个键的前缀，且较长键以 0 结尾，例如本例中的第三对键 key$_3$ 0111 和 key$_4$ 0111 0，那么它们之间不存在 discriminative bit。最后一对键 key$_4$ 0111 0 和 key$_5$ 0111 10 的 discriminative bit 恰好位于 bit 位置 4。在这种情况下，三个 discriminative bit 使 partial key mask 0010 1 中有两个 bit 被设为 1。

> **图 4.** 为二进制字符串键 {0100 01, 0100 1, 0111, 0111 0, 0111 10} 生成 partial key mask 和 partial key 的示例。这些键生成 partial key mask 0010 10，以及 partial key {00, 01, 10, 10, 11}。

为了提取 partial key，我们遍历字符串键并执行 parallel bit extract 操作。如图 4 所示，partial key 从 bit 位置 2 和 4 上的 bit 中选取。当某个 bit 位置超出键的边界时，例如 key$_3$ 的情况，我们会在逻辑上用 0 对其进行填充。由于 key$_3$ 和 key$_4$ 没有产生 discriminative bit，它们的 partial key 相同。查询时，我们将二者视为同一个键。

*点查询。* 在紧凑 segment 表示上执行点查询非常高效。与 point filter 类似，对不存在键的点查询通常无需对 LSM-tree 执行任何 I/O，即可返回 key-does-not-exist；当发生 false positive 时，有时需要发出 I/O 来验证该键是否存在。紧凑 segment 表示保证，在这种情况下只需一次 I/O。对已存在键的点查询最多使用一次 I/O 即可定位该键。

**算法 2：在紧凑 segment 表示上执行点查询的算法**

```text
1: function Point_Query(search_key, pkeys, pkmask)
2:    pkey ← bit_extract(search_key, pkmask)
3:    pos ← find_exact_match(pkeys, pkey)
4:    if pos = Not found then
5:        return Not found
6:    result_key ← string_key_at(pos)
7:    cmp ← str_cmp(probe_key, search_key)
8:    if cmp = 0 then
9:        return pos
10:   return Not found
```

算法 2 展示了点查询的执行过程。给定一个搜索键，第一步是使用 partial key mask 提取该搜索键的 partial key。我们采用与提取已有 partial key 相同的方式，从搜索键中提取 partial key。如果搜索键短于 discriminative bit vector，就在逻辑上用 0 填充搜索键尾部。这样会得到一个与已存储 partial key 长度相同的 partial key。

随后，我们在 partial key 数组中搜索与搜索键所提取的 partial key 完全匹配的条目。如果没有找到完全匹配，就可以在不访问 segment 内任何键的情况下断定搜索键不存在。否则，存在一个 partial key 与搜索键的 partial key 相匹配。接下来，如算法 2 第 6 行所示，我们发出一次 I/O，访问对应位置上的字符串键。只有当结果键与搜索键匹配时，才返回结果键的索引。

*范围查询。* 在紧凑 segment 表示上执行范围查询需要额外步骤。与点查询类似，范围查询的第一步也是从用户提供的搜索键中提取 partial key。然后，以该 partial key 为搜索键，在 partial key 数组中执行 longest prefix match（LPM）。该操作返回 partial key 数组中，与用户输入所提取的 partial key 前缀匹配程度最高的 partial key 的位置。与点查询类似，我们会发出一次 I/O，访问该位置上的字符串键，并将其与搜索键比较。如果搜索键恰好与所访问的字符串键匹配，就直接返回结果位置。否则，需要评估这两个键与 partial key mask 中多少个 bit 相匹配。类似于第 3.2.1 节中的直观说明，我们可以根据匹配 bit 的数量，以及所访问字符串键与搜索键之间的顺序关系，推导出一个更新后的 partial key。最后，使用更新后的搜索键 partial key 在 partial key 上执行常规搜索，即可找到结果位置。在紧凑 segment 表示上执行查询的详细过程见算法 3 和算法 4。

**算法 3：在紧凑 segment 表示上执行范围查询的算法**

```text
1: function Range_Query(search_key, pkeys, pkmask)
2:    pkey ← bit_extract(search_key, pkmask)
3:    pos ← longest_prefix_match(pkeys, pkey)
4:    probe_key ← string_key_at(pos)
5:    cmp ← str_cmp(probe_key, search_key)
6:    if cmp = 0 then
7:        return pos
8:    n_mismatch ← mismatch_count(probe_key, search_key, pkmask)
9:    if mismatch_count = 0 then
10:       if cmp < 0 then
11:           return pos
12:       else
13:           return pos + 1
14:   if cmp < 0 then
15:       new_pkey ← pkey_lowest(pkey, n_mismatch)
16:   else
17:       new_pkey ← pkey_highest(pkey, n_mismatch)
18:   pos ← pkey_search(pkeys, new_pkey)
19:   return pos
```

下面几段详细讨论如何使用 partial key mask 和 partial key 定位搜索键。为简单起见，我们将 seek 操作定义为查找大于或等于搜索键的最小键，并假设每个范围查询都以一次 seek 操作开始。执行范围查询的第一步与点查询相似。不同之处在于，提取 partial key 后，我们可能找不到与搜索键 partial key 完全匹配的 partial key。即使确实找到了匹配的 partial key，结果位置处的字符串键也可能与搜索键不匹配，即发生 “false positive”。在这两种情况下，我们仍希望用最少的 I/O 完成范围查询。为此，在 partial key 中搜索时，需要模拟 radix tree（trie）上的匹配过程，从而找到 LPM partial key。

确定 LPM partial key 的位置后，我们会读取 segment 中同一位置上的实际字符串键，后文将其称为 probe key。这是唯一发出 I/O 并执行字符串比较的地方。得到实际字符串键后，可以判断它是否完全匹配。如果完全匹配，就立即返回该位置。否则，我们可以知道 probe key 大于还是小于搜索键。

访问实际字符串键后，不仅可以推断搜索键与 probe key 的顺序，还能知道两者之间匹配的字符或 bit 数量。最长公共前缀（longest common prefix，LCP）的长度恰好能够刻画这一指标。可以将一个 segment 中的所有键视为组织在 trie 中：每个内部节点代表其所有叶节点的一个公共前缀，而以该节点为根的子树中的所有键都共享该节点的公共前缀。对搜索键执行 seek，等价于查找该子树的最左叶节点，或者最右叶节点之后的下一个键。如果 probe key 大于 search tree，就需要查找最左叶节点；否则，probe key 小于 search tree，我们要查找最右叶节点之后的下一个键。

直接在真实键上执行范围查询会触发过多 I/O。相反，我们构造一个新的 partial key；使用它在 partial key 中进行搜索时，会返回相同的位置。为此，第一步是找出 LCP 与 partial key mask 匹配的 bit 数量。为了便于下一步处理，实现中计算的是不匹配 bit 的数量，如算法 4 的 mismatch_count 函数所示。如果不匹配 bit 的数量为 0，就意味着搜索键和 probe key 中有一个是另一个的前缀。该函数根据键比较结果返回当前位置或下一个位置。

**算法 4：范围查询的辅助函数**

```text
function mismatch_count(probe_key, search_key, pkmask)
   lcp_bit ← bitewise_lcp(probe_key, search_key)
   matched_bits ← pop_count(pkmask[: lcp_bit])
   tot_ones ← pop_count(pkmask)
   return tot_ones - matched_bits

function pkey_highest(pkey, mismatch_count)
   bit ← shift_left(1, mismatch_count)
   mask ← bit - 1
   highest ← bitwise_or(highest, mask)
   highest ← highest + 1
   return highest

function pkey_lowest(pkey, mismatch_count)
   bit ← shift_left(1, mismatch_count)
   mask ← reverse_bit(bit - 1)
   if pkey < bit then
       return 0
   lowest ← bitwise_and(pkey, mask)
   return lowest
```

第二步中，我们将使用不匹配 bit 的数量构造一个新的 partial key，使其能够指向最左叶节点，或者该子树最右叶节点之后的键。如果搜索键大于 probe key，我们将构造一个新的 partial key，将搜索键 partial key 中所有尾部不匹配 bit 都填为 0，如函数 pkey_lowest 所示。否则，就将它们全部填为 1，然后再进行算术加一。随后，使用新的 partial key 在 partial key 中执行 seek 操作。该搜索得到的位置保证就是在 segment 中对搜索键执行 seek 操作所得到的位置。

> **图 5.** 使用 radix tree（trie）说明 Disco 范围查询的示例。

*范围查询示例。* 为了更好地说明范围查询如何模拟 trie 上的搜索，我们使用图 4 中的示例键，在图 5 中构建了一棵 Patricia trie。给定搜索键 0101 10 和 partial key mask 0010 10，可以得到搜索键的 partial key 01。接下来，我们尝试找到 LPM partial key，这等价于沿 Patricia trie 前进并匹配 token。搜索到达 partial key 01，其对应的字符串键为 0100 1。算法 3 将该键记为 probe key。通过比较 probe key 与搜索键，我们得知搜索键大于 probe key，因此需要找到 LPM 子树最右叶节点之后的键。随后，我们调用算法 4 中的辅助函数 mismatch_count，求出 partial key 中不匹配 bit 的数量。结果表明，probe key 与搜索键共享 3 bit 的公共前缀；在 partial key mask 中，这只对应于匹配了一个值为 0 的 bit。因此，partial key mask 中有一个不匹配 bit。已知 probe key 小于搜索键后，我们使用算法 4 中的辅助函数 pkey_highest 构造新的 partial key。该函数将尾部不匹配 bit 填为 1，再对其加一，得到新的 partial key 10。再次使用这个新键搜索数组时，会返回数组中第三个 partial key 的位置。对于这组字符串键，查找搜索键 0101 10 所得到的结果位置确实就是该位置。

sections.title: 3.3 Rebuilding Disco
sections.originalMarkdown:
Because a Disco is an index structure built for a set of runs, whenever a new run is generated or some existing runs get replaced, the Disco needs to be updated accordingly. One straightforward way to update a Disco is to build a new Disco. Given a new set of sorted runs, we can build a Disco by scanning and sort-merging all the keys in those runs. Although the write I/O could be kept small because of the compact design, scanning all data in those runs can incur excessive read I/O that might slow down the building process and the overall write performance. In this section, we analyze the rebuilding cost, discuss the trade-off we make in Disco, and show the techniques we employ to reduce the rebuilding cost. We will evaluate how much read and write I/O Disco triggers during write intensive workloads and how Disco affects the write performance in Section 4.2.

*Fig. 6. A diagram illustrating how to use compaction history for building Disco. In this diagram two old runs (R1 and R2) get merged and become new R1. R’ S’ denotes Run Selectors.*

*Disco reduces compaction sort-merging overhead.* LSM-trees maintain background compaction processes that periodically organize sorted runs to maintain its structures. In a Disco-indexed LSM-tree, such as DiscoDB, the existing runs are already indexed by an existing Disco. When doing compactions, the existing Disco can help accelerate sort-merging without excessive in-memory comparisons.

*Building new Disco makes use of compaction history.* After a compaction, the old Disco is obsolete and needs to be replaced. The cost for building the new Disco would be prohibitively high if we naively scan all the runs to build the new Disco. However, we observe that compactions only rewrite existing keys in the old runs onto the new runs and the metadata in the new Disco mostly stay the same as the old one except that the run selectors and the cursor offsets for the merged runs are updated. Based on this, we implement a mechanism for building the new Disco from the old Disco with updated run selectors and cursor offsets.

Figure 6 shows a diagram of the mechanism. The compaction process generates and ships a compaction history to the Disco building process. The data structure of compaction history is lightweight. It describes what keys get merged in the new run and the corresponding cursor offset changes. The Disco building process uses the compaction history to update the run selectors and cursor offsets without reading the new set of runs. Because compactions in LSM-trees inevitably need to load the to-be-merged runs to memory to perform merges, the I/O cost of rebuilding Disco only includes reading the old Disco and writing the new one.

*Unaffected segments do not trigger I/O.* LSM-trees generate a new run for a batch of updates. After a new run is generated, the old Disco needs to be updated to incorporate the new run into the index. Directly scanning the new set of runs to build the Disco can cause excessive read I/O. The problem is prominent especially when a small run is generated and the Disco has to be rebuilt on a large amount of existing data. However, we find in this situation that most of the segments in the new Disco only need to update the cursor offsets for the new run and only a small percentage of the segments have to be rebuilt by scanning. To save I/O to rebuild the new Disco, we skip accessing the segments that are not affected by the updates.

When there is a new run generated, rebuilding a new Disco can be thought of as inserting the new keys to the existing Disco. To avoid scanning the existing runs, we try to maintain the existing search tree in Disco as much as possible. We use the search tree to find which segment each new key belongs to. If a segment does not receive any new keys, all the segment metadata stay the same except for cursor offsets. In this case, we do not need to issue any I/Os to the underlying runs. The cursor offset of the new run will be appended to the new Disco. When a segment is affected by the new keys, we try to incorporate them in the segment if the lengths of partial keys are still within threshold; otherwise, the segment is split to incorporate the new keys. In this way, the read I/O to rebuild the Disco only depends on the update size and not the existing data size.

*Batch I/O reduces rebuilding cost.* If scanning existing runs is unavoidable to rebuild Disco, the I/O cost can still stay low. Because using scans to build Disco has a sequential access pattern, the read I/O is less expensive by doing I/O in batches. This can be easily implemented by calling `posix_fadvise` and `posix_madvise` to change the read-ahead window before and after building Disco. Moreover, implementations can choose to rebuild Disco asynchronously so that Disco does not block front-end write and provides efficient read subsequently.
sections.translationMarkdown:
由于 Disco 是为一组 run 构建的索引结构，因此每当生成新的 run 或替换某些现有 run 时，都需要相应地更新 Disco。更新 Disco 的一种直接方法是构建一个新的 Disco。给定一组新的 sorted run，我们可以扫描这些 run 中的所有键并对其执行排序归并，从而构建 Disco。尽管得益于紧凑的设计，写 I/O 可以保持在较低水平，但扫描这些 run 中的所有数据会产生大量读 I/O，可能拖慢构建过程和整体写性能。本节分析重建成本，讨论我们在 Disco 中作出的权衡，并介绍用于降低重建成本的技术。我们将在第 4.2 节评估 Disco 在写密集型工作负载中触发的读写 I/O 量，以及 Disco 对写性能的影响。

*图 6. 使用 compaction history 构建 Disco 的示意图。在该图中，两个旧 run（R1 和 R2）合并后成为新的 R1。R’ S’ 表示 Run Selector。*

*Disco 降低 compaction 排序归并开销。* LSM-tree 维护后台 compaction 进程，周期性地组织 sorted run，以维持其结构。在使用 Disco 索引的 LSM-tree（例如 DiscoDB）中，现有 run 已由现有 Disco 建立索引。执行 compaction 时，现有 Disco 可以帮助加速排序归并，而无需进行大量内存内比较。

*构建新 Disco 会利用 compaction history。* compaction 完成后，旧 Disco 已经过时，需要被替换。如果直接扫描所有 run 来构建新 Disco，其成本将高得难以接受。然而，我们观察到，compaction 只是将旧 run 中已有的键重写到新 run；新 Disco 中的大部分 metadata 与旧 Disco 保持相同，只有已合并 run 的 run selector 和游标偏移需要更新。基于这一点，我们实现了一种机制：更新 run selector 和游标偏移，并由旧 Disco 构建新 Disco。

图 6 展示了该机制。compaction 进程生成 compaction history，并将其传递给 Disco 构建进程。compaction history 的数据结构非常轻量，它描述了哪些键被合并到新 run 中，以及相应的游标偏移变化。Disco 构建进程利用 compaction history 更新 run selector 和游标偏移，而无需读取新的一组 run。由于 LSM-tree 中的 compaction 不可避免地需要将待合并的 run 加载到内存中执行归并，因此重建 Disco 的 I/O 成本仅包括读取旧 Disco 和写入新 Disco。

*未受影响的 segment 不会触发 I/O。* LSM-tree 会为一批更新生成一个新 run。生成新 run 后，需要更新旧 Disco，将新 run 纳入索引。直接扫描新的一组 run 来构建 Disco 可能产生大量读 I/O。当生成的 run 很小、而 Disco 必须基于大量现有数据重建时，这一问题尤其突出。然而，我们发现在这种情况下，新 Disco 中的大多数 segment 只需更新新 run 的游标偏移，只有一小部分 segment 必须通过扫描来重建。为了节省重建新 Disco 所需的 I/O，我们会跳过访问不受这些更新影响的 segment。

生成新 run 时，重建新 Disco 可以视为将新键插入现有 Disco。为了避免扫描现有 run，我们尽可能维持 Disco 中原有的搜索树。我们使用搜索树确定每个新键所属的 segment。如果一个 segment 没有接收到任何新键，那么除游标偏移外，该 segment 的所有 metadata 都保持不变。在这种情况下，我们不需要向底层 run 发出任何 I/O。新 run 的游标偏移会被追加到新 Disco 中。当某个 segment 受到新键影响时，如果 partial key 的长度仍在阈值以内，我们就尝试将这些键纳入该 segment；否则，拆分该 segment 以容纳新键。通过这种方式，重建 Disco 所需的读 I/O 仅取决于更新大小，而不取决于现有数据大小。

*批量 I/O 降低重建成本。* 如果重建 Disco 时无法避免扫描现有 run，I/O 成本仍然可以保持较低。由于通过扫描构建 Disco 呈现顺序访问模式，批量执行 I/O 可以降低读 I/O 的成本。这很容易实现：在构建 Disco 前后调用 `posix_fadvise` 和 `posix_madvise`，以调整预读窗口。此外，实现者可以选择异步重建 Disco，使 Disco 不会阻塞前端写入，并能在随后提供高效读取。

sections.title: 3.4 Storage Cost of Disco
sections.originalMarkdown:
The storage cost of Disco reflects both read and write efficiency. A smaller storage cost means a lower cost to rebuild the Disco and a high cache utilization when serving queries. The metadata of Disco can be broken down into five parts: anchor keys, cursor offsets, run selectors, partial key mask, and partial keys. We use $D$ to denote the number of keys in a segment which is also referred as the segment size. The anchor key takes about $1/D$ of the total key size on overage. If we assume the size of a cursor offset is $S$ bytes and the number of runs is $H$, the cursor offsets in a segment in Disco takes $S \times H$ bytes while a run selector requires $\lceil\log_2(H)\rceil$ bits. Because the partial key mask record the bit positions where the neighboring keys differ, without any encoding optimizations a partial key mask is expected to require the average key size $\bar{L}$ of space in a segment. We assume each partial key takes $P$ bytes. Adding all the five parts together, Disco is expected to use

$$
\frac{2\bar{L} + SH}{D} + \frac{\lceil\log_2(H)\rceil}{8} + P \quad \text{bytes/key}.
$$

We estimate the actual storage cost by plugging practical numbers into the equation. We use cursor offset of 4 bytes ($S = 4$), enabling it to address 4 GB of space in a run. We evaluate the situation where the Disco indexes 8 sorted runs ($H = 8$). A practical implementation would use 1 byte for a run selector which can index 256 runs at most. Combining all the configurations, we have the storage cost of $(2\bar{L} + 32)/D + 1 + P$ bytes per key. A practical configuration chooses the partial key size from 16-bit, 32-bit, and 64-bit integers, corresponding to $P = 2$, $P = 4$, and $P = 8$ respectively. The choice of partial key size affects the average segment size. We will use 32-bit partial keys ($P = 8$) as the typical configuration. Section 3.5 takes a more in-depth discussion on the segment sizes and the partial key sizes.

Table 1 presents a summary of the storage costs of Disco in comparisons with REMIX and SSTable. We use the average key and value sizes collected in production environments [2, 9, 19]. For SSTable, we compare to Bloom filters configured to 10 bits per key [22]. The block index [20, 26] is estimated as the average key-value size plus a block handle size (4 B) divide the number of key-value pairs in a 4 KB block. The storage cost of REMIX is calculated according to the configurations in [53]. The last column shows the size ratio of Disco to all the key-value data it indexes. The storage cost of Disco is up to 29% of the key-value data when the value size is small such as in USR store. For most of the key-value stores, Disco takes less than 5% of the key-value data.

*Table 1. Comparison of Disco storage cost to SSTable and REMIX with real-world key-value sizes. BI stands for Block Index. BF stands for Bloom Filter. The last column shows the size ratio of Disco to the key-value data. For Disco when $P = 2$, $D = 24$; $P = 4$, $D = 64$; $P = 8$, $D = 256$.*

Disco 的存储成本同时反映了读效率和写效率。存储成本越小，重建 Disco 的成本越低，并且在处理查询时缓存利用率越高。Disco 的 metadata 可以分为五部分：锚点键、游标偏移、run selector、partial key mask 和 partial key。我们用 $D$ 表示一个 segment 中的键数，也称为 segment 大小。平均而言，锚点键约占总键大小的 $1/D$。假设一个游标偏移的大小为 $S$ 字节、run 的数量为 $H$，则 Disco 的一个 segment 中，游标偏移占用 $S \times H$ 字节，而一个 run selector 需要 $\lceil\log_2(H)\rceil$ bit。由于 partial key mask 记录相邻键之间存在差异的 bit 位置，在不采用任何编码优化的情况下，一个 segment 中的 partial key mask 预计需要占用相当于平均键大小 $\bar{L}$ 的空间。假设每个 partial key 占用 $P$ 字节。将这五部分相加，Disco 预计每个键占用

$$
\frac{2\bar{L} + SH}{D} + \frac{\lceil\log_2(H)\rceil}{8} + P \quad \text{字节/键}。
$$

我们将实际参数代入公式，以估算实际存储成本。游标偏移采用 4 字节（$S = 4$），因此能够寻址一个 run 中 4 GB 的空间。我们评估 Disco 为 8 个 sorted run（$H = 8$）建立索引的情况。实际实现会用 1 字节存储 run selector，最多可以索引 256 个 run。综合所有配置后，每个键的存储成本为 $(2\bar{L} + 32)/D + 1 + P$ 字节。实际配置会从 16-bit、32-bit 和 64-bit 整数中选择 partial key 的大小，分别对应 $P = 2$、$P = 4$ 和 $P = 8$。partial key 大小的选择会影响平均 segment 大小。我们将使用 32-bit partial key（$P = 8$）作为典型配置。第 3.5 节将更深入地讨论 segment 大小和 partial key 大小。

表 1 总结了 Disco 的存储成本，并将其与 REMIX 和 SSTable 进行了比较。我们使用从生产环境收集的平均键大小和值大小 [2, 9, 19]。对于 SSTable，我们比较配置为每个键 10 bit 的 Bloom filter [22]。Block Index [20, 26] 的存储成本估算方式为：将平均键值大小与 block handle 大小（4 B）相加，再除以一个 4 KB block 中的键值对数量。REMIX 的存储成本依据 [53] 中的配置计算。最后一列给出了 Disco 大小与其所索引的全部键值数据大小之比。当值很小时（例如 USR store），Disco 的存储成本最高可达到键值数据的 29%。对于大多数 key-value store，Disco 占键值数据的比例小于 5%。
