sections.title: 3.5 Implementations
sections.originalMarkdown:
The algorithms and examples presented in previous sections give a high-level idea of our design. We did not mention how the operations on Disco tries are performed and how we apply them to build an LSM-tree based database. In this section, we will fill those blanks by providing some implementation details that could help practitioners implement efficient key-value store systems using Disco.

*Partial key lengths.* The partial key lengths directly determine the storage cost of Disco because we need to store one partial key for every key. It also indirectly affects the storage cost because it determines the maximum segment size. By the number of combinations of fixed length bit strings, a $n$ bit partial key could have at most $2^n$ unique partial keys. It is obvious that $n$ bit partial key mask could generate at least $n + 1$ unique partial keys. Depending on the datasets, $n$ bit partial keys can encode from $n + 1$ to $2^n$ keys.

To see how partial key lengths affect the maximum segment sizes we use some real-world datasets[^2] and build Disco by incorporating keys into a segment until the partial keys cannot differentiate new keys. We use the partial key lengths of 16 bits, 32 bits, and 64 bits. We then measure the segment sizes for some real-world datasets. We find that 32-bit partial keys could distinguish about 50 to 400 string keys on average. 16-bit partial keys have similar segment size distributions with the mean values ranging from 21 to 50. The mean values for 64-bit partial keys are much larger, reaching the magnitude of 1,000.

Based on that, we find that it is a better trade-off to use 32-bit partial keys in a practical implementation. With 32-bit, at least 33 string keys could be distinguished. It guarantees that the search tree picks an anchor key for at least every 33 keys, so the search tree could be kept compact. With 32-bit partial keys, it introduces a modest storage cost of 4 bytes per key. In addition, because 32-bit integers are ubiquitous in varies of hardware platforms, bitwise operations could be performed most efficiently.

*Bit extractions.* As shown in Algorithms 1, 2, and 3, both the build and query processeses involve bit extraction operations. The efficiency of bit extraction operations are the key to achieve efficient operations on Disco. In our implementation, we divide the strings into 8 bytes units to cut down the number of iterations for long strings. We also use the PEXT instruction [11] in BMI2 instruction set available on modern CPUs to perform efficient extractions.

*The optimized file layout.* The design goal of Disco is to achieve I/O efficient queries. It is critical for a Disco file itself to be I/O efficient. We store Disco in a B$^+$-tree like layout. The node sizes are aligned to 4 KB blocks, the most commonly used block I/O unit. Each anchor key points to a position that stores all the metadata of a segment. Metadata blocks are also aligned to 4 KB blocks to prevent the segment metadata from being fragmented into two different I/O blocks.

*The LSM-tree structure.* We build an LSM-tree based key-value store, DiscoDB, to showcase the effectiveness of Disco. DiscoDB divides the keys into partitions. Each partition consists of a set of overlapping runs that are indexed by a Disco. Similar to other traditional LSM-tree based indexes, it buffers updates in memory and periodically flushes them to respective partitions. When trying to flush data from memory to partitions on disk, DiscoDB will reject flushing to a partition and send the data back to memory if the updates are too small to maintain the cost of rebuilding Disco low. The Disco of a partition will be rebuilt when a new run is flushed to a partition from MemTable. DiscoDB also maintains a background compaction process that merges selected runs based on the estimated compaction cost.

[^2]: Including email addresses, amazon reviews, DBLP titles, DBLP URLs, and words in English dictionary.
sections.translationMarkdown:
前面各节给出的算法和示例从较高层次展示了我们的设计。我们尚未说明如何执行 Disco trie 上的操作，以及如何将其用于构建基于 LSM-tree 的数据库。本节将给出一些实现细节来补充这些内容，以帮助实践者使用 Disco 实现高效的 key-value store 系统。

*Partial key 长度。* partial key 长度直接决定 Disco 的存储成本，因为每个键都需要存储一个 partial key。它还会通过决定最大 segment 大小，间接影响存储成本。根据定长 bit 串的组合数，一个 $n$ bit partial key 最多可以有 $2^n$ 个不同的 partial key。显然，一个 $n$ bit partial key mask 至少可以生成 $n + 1$ 个不同的 partial key。根据数据集的不同，$n$ bit partial key 可以编码 $n + 1$ 到 $2^n$ 个键。

为了观察 partial key 长度如何影响最大 segment 大小，我们使用了一些真实世界数据集[^2]，并通过不断向一个 segment 中加入键来构建 Disco，直到 partial key 无法再区分新键。我们采用 16 bit、32 bit 和 64 bit 三种 partial key 长度，然后测量若干真实世界数据集上的 segment 大小。我们发现，32-bit partial key 平均可以区分约 50 到 400 个字符串键。16-bit partial key 的 segment 大小分布相似，其均值在 21 到 50 之间。64-bit partial key 的均值大得多，达到了 1,000 的数量级。

基于上述结果，我们发现，在实际实现中使用 32-bit partial key 能取得更好的权衡。使用 32 bit 时，至少可以区分 33 个字符串键。这保证搜索树至少每 33 个键选择一个锚点键，从而使搜索树保持紧凑。32-bit partial key 带来的存储成本较为适中，每个键占用 4 字节。此外，由于 32-bit 整数在各种硬件平台上都很常见，因此能够最高效地执行 bitwise operation。

*Bit 提取。* 如算法 1、2 和 3 所示，构建和查询过程都涉及 bit 提取操作。bit 提取操作的效率是实现 Disco 高效操作的关键。在我们的实现中，我们将字符串划分为 8 字节单元，以减少处理长字符串时的迭代次数。我们还使用现代 CPU 所支持的 BMI2 指令集中的 PEXT 指令 [11] 来高效地执行提取。

*优化后的文件布局。* Disco 的设计目标是实现 I/O 高效的查询，因此 Disco 文件本身的 I/O 效率至关重要。我们采用类似 B$^+$-tree 的布局来存储 Disco。节点大小与 4 KB block 对齐，这是最常用的 block I/O 单元。每个锚点键指向一个存储该 segment 全部 metadata 的位置。Metadata block 同样与 4 KB block 对齐，以防止一个 segment 的 metadata 被拆分到两个不同的 I/O block 中。

*LSM-tree 结构。* 我们构建了一个基于 LSM-tree 的 key-value store DiscoDB，用于展示 Disco 的有效性。DiscoDB 将键划分到不同 partition 中。每个 partition 由一组彼此重叠、并由一个 Disco 建立索引的 run 组成。与其他传统的 LSM-tree 索引类似，它在内存中缓冲更新，并周期性地将更新 flush 到相应的 partition。当尝试将数据从内存 flush 到磁盘上的 partition 时，如果更新量太小，无法将 Disco 的重建成本维持在较低水平，DiscoDB 会拒绝向该 partition 执行 flush，并将数据送回内存。当一个新 run 从 MemTable flush 到某个 partition 时，该 partition 的 Disco 会被重建。DiscoDB 还维护一个后台 compaction 进程，根据估算的 compaction 成本合并选定的 run。

[^2]: 包括电子邮件地址、Amazon 评论、DBLP 标题、DBLP URL 和英语词典中的单词。

sections.title: 4 EVALUATION
sections.originalMarkdown:
In this section, we evaluate the performance of Disco and DiscoDB and compare them against traditional LSM-tree and REMIX as well as the KV-store systems derived from them, namely RocksDB and RemixDB. To further show the trade-off of Disco, we compare with a baseline LSM-tree index implementation, which we coined “Full-Index”, that organizes all the keys and pointers to the key-values on a LSM-tree in a B+-tree. We also provide another implementation, referred to as “No-Index”, that ablates the index component of DiscoDB to show the overhead of maintaining indexes.

We first evaluate the numbers of I/O operations (I/Os below) per query using micro-benchmarks to see how Disco saves I/Os. We then show the overall read and write performance of the KV-stores using workloads that emulate real-world scenarios, as well as the standardized YCSB benchmark suite.
sections.translationMarkdown:
在本节中，我们评估 Disco 和 DiscoDB 的性能，并将它们与传统 LSM-tree、REMIX，以及分别由它们衍生出的 KV-store 系统 RocksDB 和 RemixDB 进行比较。为了进一步展示 Disco 的权衡，我们还将其与一个基线 LSM-tree 索引实现进行比较。我们将该实现称为“Full-Index”，它使用 B+-tree 组织 LSM-tree 上的所有 key 以及指向 key-value 的指针。我们还提供了另一个称为“No-Index”的实现；它移除了 DiscoDB 的索引组件，用于展示维护索引所带来的开销。

我们首先使用 micro-benchmark 评估每次查询的 I/O 操作数（下文简称 I/Os），以了解 Disco 如何节省 I/O。随后，我们使用模拟真实场景的工作负载以及标准化的 YCSB benchmark suite，展示这些 KV-store 的总体读写性能。

sections.title: 4.1 Micro-benchmark Experiments
sections.originalMarkdown:
To evaluate Disco performance, we implement a micro-benchmark framework that measures I/Os per query of sorted runs. These experiments have the same setup as the motivational experiment in Section 2. They simulate the partitions of KV-stores as a set of sorted runs filled with keys that are randomly selected from an email address dataset.

Together, the partitions contain 1.8 million email address keys, which together with 120 byte values form 256 MB of key-value data. We consider point queries (probes), which return the existence or non-existence of a given query key as well as range queries (seeks) which find the smallest key greater than or equal to the query key. The experiments issue queries using a uniform random key distribution and record the I/O traces. The I/O traces are later replayed on a cache simulator that implements a classic least recently used (LRU) cache replacement policy with different cache sizes ranging from 0.1% to 100% of the dataset size.

In the micro-benchmark experiments, we compare the number of I/Os per query of Disco to that of REMIX and LSM-tree. We also include the results of B+-tree for reference. To compare these data structures fairly, we use the same 256 MB key-value pairs for all. The B+-tree organizes all data in a single sorted file while LSM-tree, REMIX, and Disco use 8 sorted runs.

**Point query experiments.** In the first set of experiments, we focus on the effectiveness of Disco in reducing the number of I/Os associated with point queries. We measure and compare the average I/Os per point query of Disco, REMIX, LSM-tree, and B+-tree. The LSM-tree includes a Bloom filter with each sorted run. According to a well accepted production configuration [22], the Bloom filters are configured to use 10 bits per key to achieve a false positive rate of 1%. The experiments use a workload of a mix of about 75% existing and 25% non-existing keys. The point query only checks for the existence of the given key.

Figure 7 shows the results. For the LSM-tree, when the cache is too small to fit all Bloom filters, the Bloom filter approach uses many more I/Os per query than all other methods. As the cache size increases to 2% of the dataset size, where most Bloom filters could fit in the cache, the LSM-tree only takes about one I/O per query to load the target key from disk. In comparison, REMIX uses as many as 4 I/Os to finish a query. This is because REMIX has to issue I/Os to read and compare $\log_2 N$ keys, where $N$ is the number of keys in a segment. In our experiment setup where $N = 32$, REMIX is expected to perform at most 5 string comparisons to locate a key. Meanwhile, Disco and B+-tree consistently maintain less than 2 I/Os per query across all cache sizes. When the cache is limited, Disco and B+-tree both use 2 I/Os (or less) to finish a query. For Disco, these 2 I/Os include one access to the Disco metadata plus one access to the probe key at line 6 in Algorithm 2. The final key comparison results in a cache hit because the data block has been loaded into the cache when accessing the probe key. For the given dataset, the B+-tree has height 2. For very small cache sizes, the 2 I/Os for B+-tree can be explained by one I/O that accesses the internal nodes and another I/O that accesses the leaf nodes. For larger cache sizes, the internal nodes are typically in the cache, and B+ tree incurs one I/O per query.

*Fig. 7. Comparisons of numbers of I/Os per point query (probe) for LSM-tree, B+-tree, REMIX, and Disco with different caching budgets. The LSM-tree, REMIX, and Disco have 8 sorted runs.*

We conclude from these results that Disco is effective in reducing the I/Os of point queries. Even though Disco has a structure similar to REMIX, we see that Disco consistently uses fewer I/Os to finish queries than REMIX across all cache budgets. The I/Os per query of Disco even approach that of the B+-tree for parts of cache size range.

**Range query experiments.** In the second set of experiments, we look at how effective Disco is in minimizing range query I/Os. Similar to the point query experiments above, these experiments also use a workload of a mix of existing and non-existing keys. However, instead of reporting on the existence of the key, range queries find the smallest key that is greater than the key given by the user. This requirement renders the Bloom filters in LSM-tree ineffective. Similar to production LSM-tree based systems [21, 25], the LSM-tree in this experiment does a range query on each sorted run and uses a min-heap merging iterator to sort-merge the results on the fly.

Figure 8 shows the results of the range query experiment. Unsurprisingly, we see that the LSM-tree has the most I/Os per query across all memory budgets because it needs to separately search each individual run. Compared to Figure 7, we find the range query results of REMIX and B+-tree largely match the point query results. This is expected because REMIX and B+-tree do not have any specific optimizations on one type of the query and they process the point and range queries in the same way. We do see a slight increase in the I/Os per query for Disco. When the cache is limited, the I/Os per query for Disco is slightly higher than 2. In Figure 8, the gap between B+-tree and Disco is larger than the gap in Figure 7. The difference is because Disco is able to skip I/Os to empty point queries when the partial keys do not match as described in Section 3.2.

*Fig. 8. Comparisons of numbers of I/Os per range query (seek) for LSM-tree, B+-tree, REMIX, and Disco with different caching budgets. The LSM-tree, REMIX, and Disco have 8 sorted runs.*

In conclusion, these experiments show that Disco is effective in reducing the I/Os of range queries. When compared with REMIX, Disco only uses half of the I/Os to complete a query. When compared with LSM-tree, Disco reduces the number of I/Os per query by about 87.5%. The I/O saving can be observed across all cache budgets, and the saving is more prominent with a smaller cache.
sections.translationMarkdown:
为了评估 Disco 的性能，我们实现了一个 micro-benchmark 框架，用于测量查询 sorted runs 时每次查询产生的 I/Os。这些实验采用与第 2 节动机实验相同的设置。实验将 KV-store 的 partition 模拟成一组 sorted runs，其中填充的 key 从一个电子邮件地址数据集中随机选取。

所有 partition 合计包含 180 万个电子邮件地址 key；这些 key 与 120 byte 的 value 一起构成 256 MB 的 key-value 数据。我们考察 point query（probe）和 range query（seek）：前者返回给定查询 key 是否存在，后者查找大于或等于查询 key 的最小 key。实验按照均匀随机 key 分布发出查询并记录 I/O trace。随后，在一个实现了经典最近最少使用（LRU）cache replacement policy 的 cache simulator 上重放这些 I/O trace；所采用的不同 cache size 从数据集大小的 0.1% 到 100% 不等。

在 micro-benchmark 实验中，我们将 Disco 的每次查询 I/O 数与 REMIX 和 LSM-tree 进行比较。我们还列出了 B+-tree 的结果以供参考。为了公平比较这些数据结构，所有方法均使用相同的 256 MB key-value pairs。B+-tree 将所有数据组织在单个 sorted file 中，而 LSM-tree、REMIX 和 Disco 使用 8 个 sorted runs。

**Point query 实验。** 在第一组实验中，我们重点考察 Disco 在减少 point query 相关 I/O 数量方面的有效性。我们测量并比较 Disco、REMIX、LSM-tree 和 B+-tree 每次 point query 的平均 I/O 数。LSM-tree 为每个 sorted run 配置一个 Bloom filter。按照一种广泛接受的生产配置 [22]，Bloom filter 被配置为每个 key 使用 10 bits，以实现 1% 的 false positive rate。实验使用的工作负载由约 75% 的已存在 key 和 25% 的不存在 key 混合而成。Point query 只检查给定 key 是否存在。

图 7 展示了实验结果。对于 LSM-tree，当 cache 小到无法容纳所有 Bloom filter 时，Bloom filter 方法的每次查询 I/O 数远多于其他所有方法。当 cache size 增长到数据集大小的 2% 时，大部分 Bloom filter 都可以放入 cache，此时 LSM-tree 每次查询只需约一次 I/O 即可从磁盘加载目标 key。相比之下，REMIX 完成一次查询最多需要 4 次 I/O。这是因为 REMIX 必须发出 I/O 来读取并比较 $\log_2 N$ 个 key，其中 $N$ 是一个 segment 中的 key 数量。在我们的实验设置中，$N = 32$，因此 REMIX 定位一个 key 预计最多执行 5 次字符串比较。与此同时，在所有 cache size 下，Disco 和 B+-tree 的每次查询 I/O 数始终小于 2。当 cache 受限时，Disco 和 B+-tree 都使用 2 次（或更少）I/O 完成查询。对于 Disco，这 2 次 I/O 包括一次访问 Disco metadata，以及一次访问算法 2 第 6 行的 probe key。最后一次 key 比较会 cache hit，因为访问 probe key 时已将 data block 加载到 cache。对于给定数据集，B+-tree 的高度为 2。当 cache size 非常小时，B+-tree 的 2 次 I/O 可以解释为：一次 I/O 访问 internal node，另一次 I/O 访问 leaf node。当 cache size 较大时，internal node 通常位于 cache 中，B+ tree 每次查询产生一次 I/O。

*图 7. 在不同 cache budget 下，LSM-tree、B+-tree、REMIX 和 Disco 每次 point query（probe）的 I/O 数量比较。LSM-tree、REMIX 和 Disco 均有 8 个 sorted runs。*

我们根据这些结果得出结论：Disco 能够有效减少 point query 的 I/O。尽管 Disco 的结构与 REMIX 相似，但在所有 cache budget 下，Disco 完成查询所需的 I/O 始终少于 REMIX。在部分 cache size 范围内，Disco 的每次查询 I/O 数甚至接近 B+-tree。

**Range query 实验。** 在第二组实验中，我们考察 Disco 在最小化 range query I/O 方面的有效性。与上述 point query 实验类似，这些实验也使用由已存在 key 和不存在 key 混合而成的工作负载。但是，range query 并不报告 key 是否存在，而是查找大于用户所给 key 的最小 key。该要求使 LSM-tree 中的 Bloom filter 无法发挥作用。与生产环境中的 LSM-tree 系统 [21, 25] 类似，本实验中的 LSM-tree 会对每个 sorted run 执行一次 range query，并使用 min-heap merging iterator 对结果进行即时 sort-merge。

图 8 展示了 range query 实验的结果。不出所料，由于 LSM-tree 必须分别搜索每一个 run，因此在所有 memory budget 下，其每次查询 I/O 数都是最高的。与图 7 相比，我们发现 REMIX 和 B+-tree 的 range query 结果与 point query 结果基本一致。这符合预期，因为 REMIX 和 B+-tree 并未针对某一种查询进行特殊优化，而是以相同方式处理 point query 和 range query。Disco 的每次查询 I/O 数确实略有增加。当 cache 受限时，Disco 每次查询的 I/O 数略高于 2。在图 8 中，B+-tree 与 Disco 之间的差距大于图 7 中的差距。出现这种差异是因为，如第 3.2 节所述，当 partial key 不匹配时，Disco 可以跳过空 point query 所需的 I/O。

*图 8. 在不同 cache budget 下，LSM-tree、B+-tree、REMIX 和 Disco 每次 range query（seek）的 I/O 数量比较。LSM-tree、REMIX 和 Disco 均有 8 个 sorted runs。*

总之，这些实验表明 Disco 能够有效减少 range query 的 I/O。与 REMIX 相比，Disco 只需其一半的 I/O 即可完成查询。与 LSM-tree 相比，Disco 将每次查询的 I/O 数减少了约 87.5%。在所有 cache budget 下都能观察到 I/O 节省，而且 cache 越小，节省越明显。

sections.title: 4.2 DiscoDB Performance
sections.originalMarkdown:
In the following section, we evaluate the performance of DiscoDB and compare it against RemixDB, RocksDB as well as our two baseline adaptations from DiscoDB, namely Full-Index and No-Index, as described at the beginning of Section 4.

**Experimental setup.** Unless otherwise specified, the evaluations are run on CloudLab c220g2 instances. The system runs Ubuntu 22.04 LTS with Linux 5.15. It has two Intel Xeon E5-2630 CPUs and 128 GB of DRAM. The experiments run on an Intel S3500 480G SSD [30] that is formatted with an Ext4 file system.

In our experiments, we use 16-byte fixed-length keys, each having a 64-bit integer using hexadecimal encoding. We choose value size of 120 bytes to see how each system performs under the representative key-value size [19]. To simulate our target setup where a system has a different small memory budget, we use cgroups to limit the memory resource when running the experiments. This memory resource would be used by the memory component of the LSM-tree and the OS page cache. We use RocksDB v8.10.0 configured according to the official tuning guide [22]. RemixDB is configured to have segment size $D = 32$, while DiscoDB uses 32-bit partial keys. All the experiments are done using only four client threads.
sections.translationMarkdown:
在下文中，我们评估 DiscoDB 的性能，并将其与 RemixDB、RocksDB，以及第 4 节开头所述的两个从 DiscoDB 改造而来的基线 Full-Index 和 No-Index 进行比较。

**实验设置。** 除非另有说明，评估均在 CloudLab c220g2 实例上运行。系统运行 Ubuntu 22.04 LTS 和 Linux 5.15，配有两颗 Intel Xeon E5-2630 CPU 和 128 GB DRAM。实验在一块使用 Ext4 文件系统格式化的 Intel S3500 480G SSD [30] 上运行。

在实验中，我们使用 16-byte 定长 key，每个 key 都是一个采用十六进制编码的 64-bit 整数。我们选择 120 byte 的 value size，以观察各系统在具有代表性的 key-value size 下的性能 [19]。为了模拟目标设置中系统具有不同的小 memory budget 的情形，我们在运行实验时使用 cgroups 限制 memory resource。这部分 memory resource 会由 LSM-tree 的内存组件和 OS page cache 使用。我们采用 RocksDB v8.10.0，并按照官方 tuning guide [22] 进行配置。RemixDB 的 segment size 被配置为 $D = 32$，而 DiscoDB 使用 32-bit partial key。所有实验都只使用四个 client thread。

sections.title: 4.2.1 Read performance
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section shows how Disco helps minimize the query I/Os and improve the read performance of DiscoDB. To better understand how Disco helps in DiscoDB, we will be looking at the throughput of a uniformly random query workload and its average I/O per query. We then use a skewed query workload to see the performance of DiscoDB under more realistic settings. Besides the experiments on SSDs, we also study the effectiveness of Disco on a system with a faster (Optane) storage device where the I/O cost is low.

The experiments are loaded with about 1 billion key-value pairs that have 16-byte keys and 120-byte values in a randomly shuffled order. As a result, each system has about 128 GB of key-value data after loading. We then issue queries using the keys sampled from the key range using the selected distribution. We use cgroups to limit the memory to 16 GB, 32 GB, and 64 GB to simulate system setups that have limited cache resources. Before every experiment, we clear the page cache and warm it up by issuing a batch of uniformly random query until the throughput saturates. We measure the I/Os in kernel using eBPF.

**Uniformly Random Workloads.** Figures 9 and 10 show the throughput and I/O results of the range query experiments. The experiments use seek operations with the query keys selected uniformly at random. Figure 9 shows that the read throughput of DiscoDB is up to 2.3× and 2.8× as much as RemixDB and RocksDB across three different memory budgets. Having all the keys indexed by a B+-tree, Full-Index achieves a throughput closer to DiscoDB. Full-Index has a lower throughput because of the larger size of the index, which results in more I/O on average.

*Fig. 9. Throughput of range queries (seek) with a uniform workload.*

Figure 10 shows the average I/O per query. It is calculated by dividing the total read I/O, measured in the unit of bytes, by the number of queries. This experiment is performed using only one worker thread to avoid overhead of synchronization in the eBPF program. Based on the I/O traces we collected, the I/Os issued by these systems are mostly in size of of 4 KB. The results in Figure 10 show that when the systems run with a 16 G memory budget, DiscoDB uses only about 1.45 I/Os per query, whereas RemixDB and RocksDB use about 3.0 and 4.38 I/Os per query, respectively. Full-Index achieves a result close to DiscoDB at an 1.8 I/Os per query under 16 G memory budget. The No-Index results show an average I/O cost of 6.1 I/Os per query, the highest among all the systems.

*Fig. 10. Average I/O per query of range queries (seek) with a uniform workload.*

Combining the two Figures 9 and 10, we can also find that the throughput is roughly inversely proportional to the average I/O per query. This indicates that the I/O cost is the dominant cost in these systems. Hence, Disco is effective at improving the read throughput of DiscoDB in range queries by reducing the I/O it takes to finish queries.

**Point Queries.** Figure 11 shows the throughput of point query experiments. The experiments issue probe operations uniformly at random. The probe operations check for the existence of a given key. Different from the previous range query experiments, RocksDB can utilize the Bloom filters to filter out false queries. Full-Index and DiscoDB can also skip I/Os to the runs for empty queries. For this reason, RocksDB and DiscoDB have higher throughput than the range query experiments in Figure 10. RemixDB and No-Index cannot decide whether a key exists or not without scanning the runs. However, compared to seek, they can determine more quickly that a key does not exist in a given run. To summarize, Disco’s advantage in range queries also applies to point queries, albeit to a somewhat lesser extent.

*Fig. 11. Throughput of point queries (probe) with a uniform workload.*

**Skewed Workloads.** Figure 12 shows the results of range queries on a skewed query distribution that represents more realistic workloads [2, 9, 19, 24]. We use a composite-Zipfian distribution [24] that selects 1,000 hot spots from the key range uniformly at random then adds a Zipfian distribution using the YCSB standard $\theta = 0.99$. This distribution represents workloads that are less skewed than Zipfian. We run the skewed queries until the throughput is saturated.

*Fig. 12. Throughput of range queries (seek) with a skewed workload.*

Under this skewed workload, most of the hot entries would be cached in memory. The throughput is significantly higher compared to uniform workloads for all three systems. Comparing to Figure 9, the throughput of the skewed workload is more than 4× as much as that of the uniform workload. With 64 G memory budget, DiscoDB and RemixDB achieve up to 3.9× and 4.4× speedup over the uniform workloads, respectively. This shows that DiscoDB is less sensitive to the skewed workload than RemixDB. Nevertheless, the throughput of DiscoDB is higher than RemixDB and RocksDB achieving speedups up to 1.2× and 2.2×, respectively.

**Fast Storage Devices.** To see how the DiscoDB performs on systems where the I/O cost is low, we run our experiments on a system with a similar hardware configurations as the previous experiments except the storage device is an Intel Optane SSD 905P 960G [29]. Figure 13 shows the experiment results. The results show that DiscoDB still maintains the same improvements on the query throughput when compared to RemixDB and RocksDB.

*Fig. 13. Throughput of range queries (seek) with a uniform workload on an Optane drive.*
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节展示 Disco 如何帮助减少查询 I/O，并提升 DiscoDB 的读性能。为了更好地理解 Disco 如何帮助 DiscoDB，我们将考察均匀随机查询工作负载的吞吐量及其每次查询的平均 I/O。随后，我们使用偏斜查询工作负载，观察 DiscoDB 在更贴近真实情况的设置下的性能。除了 SSD 上的实验，我们还研究 Disco 在具有更快的（Optane）存储设备、因而 I/O cost 较低的系统上的有效性。

实验以随机打乱的顺序加载约 10 亿个 key-value pair，其中 key 为 16 byte，value 为 120 byte。因此，加载完成后，每个系统大约有 128 GB 的 key-value 数据。随后，我们使用按照选定分布从 key range 中采样的 key 发出查询。我们使用 cgroups 将内存限制为 16 GB、32 GB 和 64 GB，以模拟 cache resource 受限的系统设置。每次实验前，我们都会清空 page cache，并发出一批均匀随机查询进行预热，直至吞吐量饱和。我们使用 eBPF 在 kernel 中测量 I/O。

**均匀随机工作负载。** 图 9 和图 10 展示了 range query 实验的吞吐量和 I/O 结果。实验使用 seek 操作，query key 均匀随机选取。在三种不同的 memory budget 下，图 9 显示 DiscoDB 的读吞吐量最高分别可达到 RemixDB 和 RocksDB 的 2.3 倍和 2.8 倍。由于所有 key 都由 B+-tree 索引，Full-Index 获得了更接近 DiscoDB 的吞吐量。Full-Index 的吞吐量较低，是因为索引更大，导致平均产生更多 I/O。

*图 9. 均匀工作负载下 range query（seek）的吞吐量。*

图 10 展示了每次查询的平均 I/O。其计算方法是用以 byte 为单位测得的总读 I/O 除以查询数量。该实验只使用一个 worker thread，以避免 eBPF 程序中的同步开销。根据我们收集的 I/O trace，这些系统发出的 I/O 大多为 4 KB。图 10 的结果显示，当系统在 16 G memory budget 下运行时，DiscoDB 每次查询只使用约 1.45 次 I/O，而 RemixDB 和 RocksDB 每次查询分别使用约 3.0 次和 4.38 次 I/O。在 16 G memory budget 下，Full-Index 每次查询为 1.8 次 I/O，结果接近 DiscoDB。No-Index 的结果显示，其每次查询的平均 I/O cost 为 6.1 次，是所有系统中最高的。

*图 10. 均匀工作负载下 range query（seek）的每次查询平均 I/O。*

结合图 9 和图 10 还可以发现，吞吐量大致与每次查询的平均 I/O 成反比。这说明 I/O cost 是这些系统中的主要成本。因此，通过减少完成查询所需的 I/O，Disco 能够有效提高 DiscoDB 在 range query 中的读吞吐量。

**Point Query。** 图 11 展示了 point query 实验的吞吐量。实验均匀随机地发出 probe 操作。Probe 操作用于检查给定 key 是否存在。与此前的 range query 实验不同，RocksDB 可以使用 Bloom filter 过滤掉结果为假的查询。对于空查询，Full-Index 和 DiscoDB 也可以跳过访问 runs 的 I/O。因此，RocksDB 和 DiscoDB 的吞吐量高于图 10 中的 range query 实验。RemixDB 和 No-Index 如果不扫描 runs，就无法判断一个 key 是否存在。不过，与 seek 相比，它们可以更快确定一个 key 不存在于给定 run 中。总之，Disco 在 range query 中的优势同样适用于 point query，只是程度略小。

*图 11. 均匀工作负载下 point query（probe）的吞吐量。*

**偏斜工作负载。** 图 12 展示了偏斜查询分布下的 range query 结果；该分布代表了更贴近真实情况的工作负载 [2, 9, 19, 24]。我们使用 composite-Zipfian 分布 [24]：首先从 key range 中均匀随机选择 1,000 个 hot spot，然后加入采用 YCSB 标准参数 $\theta = 0.99$ 的 Zipfian 分布。该分布所代表的工作负载比 Zipfian 的偏斜程度更低。我们持续运行偏斜查询，直到吞吐量饱和。

*图 12. 偏斜工作负载下 range query（seek）的吞吐量。*

在这种偏斜工作负载下，大多数 hot entry 会被缓存在内存中。对于所有三个系统，其吞吐量都显著高于均匀工作负载。与图 9 相比，偏斜工作负载的吞吐量超过均匀工作负载的 4 倍。在 64 G memory budget 下，相对于均匀工作负载，DiscoDB 和 RemixDB 分别获得最高 3.9 倍和 4.4 倍的加速。这表明 DiscoDB 对偏斜工作负载的敏感性低于 RemixDB。尽管如此，DiscoDB 的吞吐量仍高于 RemixDB 和 RocksDB，最高分别实现 1.2 倍和 2.2 倍的加速。

**快速存储设备。** 为了观察 DiscoDB 在 I/O cost 较低的系统上的表现，我们在一个与此前实验硬件配置相似的系统上运行实验，唯一的区别是存储设备换成了 Intel Optane SSD 905P 960G [29]。图 13 展示了实验结果。结果表明，与 RemixDB 和 RocksDB 相比，DiscoDB 在查询吞吐量上仍保持了相同的提升。

*图 13. Optane drive 上均匀工作负载下 range query（seek）的吞吐量。*

sections.title: 4.2.2 Write performance
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section investigates the effect of Disco on the write performance of DiscoDB. In these experiments, we load each system with 1 billion key-value pairs in a shuffled order and measure the throughput and total read and write I/O issued during the experiments. Each key-value pair has 16-byte key and 120-byte value. As a result, each system has in a total of 128 GB of key-value pairs after loading. Similarly to the previous experiments, we limit the available memory for the systems to 16 GB, 32 GB, and 64 GB to see how they perform under different memory budgets. The experiment results are shown in Figures 14 and 15.

*Fig. 14. Throughput of loading 1 billion key-value pairs in a shuffled order.*

The results show that the write throughput of DiscoDB is 2% to 10% lower than RemixDB across different memory budgets. This is slowdown is mostly due to the need to construct partial keys for rebuilding Disco and the extra write to persist them in DiscoDB. Nonetheless, the write throughput of DiscoDB is still 1.9× to 2.1× as high as RocksDB in these experiments. Bearing the cost of maintaining Disco, loading in DiscoDB is about 50% slower than No-Index, where no indexes are maintained at all. When compared with Full-Index which organizes all keys in a B+-tree, DiscoDB is 2× as fast in loading.

Figure 15 shows the write and read I/O used by these systems in the loading experiments. The write I/O results show that DiscoDB uses 16% more write I/O than RemixDB, whereas RocksDB write I/O is 2.7× and 3.2× to that of DiscoDB and RemixDB, respectively. Comparing to Full-Index, DiscoDB uses about 30% less write I/O due to the compact design of Disco. On the other hand, the read I/O results show that DiscoDB, RemixDB, and Full-Index use 5.4× more read I/O than RocksDB and No-Index with 16 G memory budget, largely explained by the need to maintain and rebuild the index. However, for DiscoDB the extra read I/O does not reduce the loading throughput.

*Fig. 15. Total write and read I/O of the loading experiment.*

In conclusion, DiscoDB has a competitive write performance versus RemixDB and still achieves a higher write throughput than RocksDB.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节研究 Disco 对 DiscoDB 写性能的影响。在这些实验中，我们以打乱的顺序向每个系统加载 10 亿个 key-value pair，并测量实验期间的吞吐量以及发出的总读写 I/O。每个 key-value pair 的 key 为 16 byte，value 为 120 byte。因此，加载完成后，每个系统总计拥有 128 GB 的 key-value pair。与此前实验类似，我们将系统的可用内存限制为 16 GB、32 GB 和 64 GB，以观察它们在不同 memory budget 下的表现。实验结果如图 14 和图 15 所示。

*图 14. 以打乱顺序加载 10 亿个 key-value pair 的吞吐量。*

结果表明，在不同 memory budget 下，DiscoDB 的写吞吐量比 RemixDB 低 2% 到 10%。这种性能下降主要源于：重建 Disco 时需要构造 partial key，以及为了在 DiscoDB 中持久化这些 partial key 而产生的额外写入。尽管如此，在这些实验中，DiscoDB 的写吞吐量仍是 RocksDB 的 1.9 倍到 2.1 倍。由于承担了维护 Disco 的成本，DiscoDB 的加载速度比完全不维护索引的 No-Index 慢约 50%。与使用 B+-tree 组织所有 key 的 Full-Index 相比，DiscoDB 的加载速度是其 2 倍。

图 15 展示了这些系统在加载实验中使用的读写 I/O。写 I/O 结果表明，DiscoDB 使用的写 I/O 比 RemixDB 多 16%，而 RocksDB 的写 I/O 分别是 DiscoDB 和 RemixDB 的 2.7 倍和 3.2 倍。与 Full-Index 相比，由于 Disco 设计紧凑，DiscoDB 使用的写 I/O 少约 30%。另一方面，读 I/O 结果显示，在 16 G memory budget 下，DiscoDB、RemixDB 和 Full-Index 使用的读 I/O 是 RocksDB 和 No-Index 的 5.4 倍；这主要可以由维护和重建索引的需要来解释。不过，对于 DiscoDB，额外的读 I/O 并未降低加载吞吐量。

*图 15. 加载实验的总写 I/O 和总读 I/O。*

总之，相较于 RemixDB，DiscoDB 具有富有竞争力的写性能，并且仍能取得高于 RocksDB 的写吞吐量。

sections.title: 4.3 YCSB
sections.originalMarkdown:
YCSB [13] is a popular benchmark suite for key-value stores. It uses synthetic workloads that emulate real-world production workload patterns with different read-write ratios. We run the YCSB workloads on DiscoDB, RemixDB, RocksDB, Full-Index, and No-Index to see how they compare in more realistic settings. For each workload, we use the 128 GB stores generated from the previous loading experiments so the keys are 16-byte fixed-length keys and values are 120 bytes long. We run each workload for 200 seconds. The details of the YCSB workloads are shown in Table 2. Except for workload D which uses the latest query distribution, all other workloads use the composite-Zipfian distribution described in the previous section. Each workload is done using four client threads.

**Table 2. YCSB workloads**

| Workload | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operations | 50% U; 50% R | 5% U; 95% R | 100% R | 5% I; 95% R | 5% I; 95% S | 50% R; 50% M |

\* I: Insert; U: Update; R: Read; S: Seek-Scan next 50 keys; M: Read-Modify-Write.

**SSD.** We run the YCSB experiments on CloudLab instances with SSD storage, described in Section 4.2. Figure 16 shows the YCSB results of experiments with memory limited to 16 GB. The results show that DiscoDB outperforms RemixDB and RocksDB in workloads A, B, C, and F that mostly consist of read operations. Because workload D uses the latest query distribution that is skewed towards recently inserted items, most of the queries are served from the MemTable of the LSM-tree, so workload D is not of interest to our analysis of Disco. Here, DiscoDB, RemixDB, Full-Index, and No-Index achieve over 2× improvements than RocksDB largely because they all employ Wormhole [50] as the MemTable. In workload E, the throughput of the other systems is lower than RocksDB. This is because workload E scans the next 50 keys after a seek, most of the query cost is dominated by the I/O to scan the keys.

*Fig. 16. YCSB results on SSD with 16 G memory limit.*

**Fast storage devices.** We repeat the same YCSB experiments on a similar system except using Intel Optane SSD as the storage device to see how the numbers change when the I/O cost is low. Figure 17 shows the experiment results. When compared with RemixDB and RocksDB, DiscoDB still has similar trends as the previous experiments on SSD in workloads A, B, C, and F. Comparing Figures 16 and 17, one noticeable difference is that RemixDB achieves higher throughput than RocksDB in these workloads. The benefits of fast range queries in RemixDB primarily apply to high speed I/O systems where the CPU cost is more dominant. Evidence of this can also be seen in the similarity between RemixDB and No-Index performance. Another interesting result is the relatively strong performance of the Full-Index design, despite its higher memory consumption. These YCSB workloads have relatively short (16-byte) keys, which limits the advantage of compactness in Disco. At the same time, Disco does incur some extra overhead compared to full-index, particularly in extracting partial keys, offsetting the smaller compactness gains.

*Fig. 17. YCSB results on an Optane drive with 16 G memory limit.*

YCSB [13] 是一种流行的 key-value store benchmark suite。它使用合成工作负载来模拟具有不同读写比例的真实生产工作负载模式。我们在 DiscoDB、RemixDB、RocksDB、Full-Index 和 No-Index 上运行 YCSB 工作负载，以观察它们在更贴近真实情况的设置下的表现。对于每个工作负载，我们使用此前加载实验生成的 128 GB store，因此 key 是 16-byte 定长 key，value 长度为 120 byte。每个工作负载运行 200 秒。YCSB 工作负载的详细信息如表 2 所示。除使用 latest query distribution 的工作负载 D 外，其他所有工作负载都使用上一节所述的 composite-Zipfian 分布。每个工作负载均使用四个 client thread 完成。

**表 2. YCSB 工作负载**

| 工作负载 | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 操作 | 50% U；50% R | 5% U；95% R | 100% R | 5% I；95% R | 5% I；95% S | 50% R；50% M |

\* I：Insert；U：Update；R：Read；S：Seek 后扫描接下来的 50 个 key；M：Read-Modify-Write。

**SSD。** 我们在第 4.2 节所述的、配备 SSD 存储的 CloudLab 实例上运行 YCSB 实验。图 16 展示了内存限制为 16 GB 时的 YCSB 实验结果。结果表明，在主要由读操作构成的工作负载 A、B、C 和 F 中，DiscoDB 的表现优于 RemixDB 和 RocksDB。由于工作负载 D 使用偏向最近插入 item 的 latest query distribution，大多数查询都由 LSM-tree 的 MemTable 提供服务，因此工作负载 D 与我们对 Disco 的分析无关。在这里，DiscoDB、RemixDB、Full-Index 和 No-Index 相比 RocksDB 均实现了超过 2 倍的提升，主要原因是它们都使用 Wormhole [50] 作为 MemTable。在工作负载 E 中，其他系统的吞吐量低于 RocksDB。这是因为工作负载 E 会在 seek 后扫描接下来的 50 个 key，大部分查询成本由扫描这些 key 的 I/O 主导。

*图 16. 16 G 内存限制下 SSD 上的 YCSB 结果。*

**快速存储设备。** 我们在相似的系统上重复相同的 YCSB 实验，但将 Intel Optane SSD 用作存储设备，以观察 I/O cost 较低时各项数值如何变化。图 17 展示了实验结果。与 RemixDB 和 RocksDB 相比，DiscoDB 在工作负载 A、B、C 和 F 中仍呈现出与此前 SSD 实验相似的趋势。比较图 16 和图 17，一个明显的区别是 RemixDB 在这些工作负载中取得了高于 RocksDB 的吞吐量。RemixDB 中快速 range query 的收益主要体现在高速 I/O 系统上，因为在此类系统中 CPU cost 更占主导。RemixDB 和 No-Index 的性能相似，也可以作为这一点的证据。另一个有趣的结果是，尽管 Full-Index 设计消耗更多内存，其性能仍然相对较强。这些 YCSB 工作负载的 key 相对较短（16 byte），限制了 Disco 在紧凑性方面的优势。同时，与 full-index 相比，Disco 确实会产生一些额外开销，尤其是提取 partial key 的开销，这抵消了较小的紧凑性收益。

*图 17. 16 G 内存限制下 Optane drive 上的 YCSB 结果。*

sections.title: 5 RELATED WORK
sections.originalMarkdown:
There exists a branch of research that uses filters to reduce the query I/Os on LSM-trees. Filters could be categorized into point filters [7, 16, 18, 52] and range filters [31, 36, 47, 51]. Filters improve query performance by skipping reading the files that the filter thinks the target key does not exist. Comparing to Disco’s indexing approach, filters become less effective when the memory budget is limited. In addition, filters do not help locate keys. Extra I/Os are needed to actually locate the keys after filtering, whereas Disco directly pinpoints the target key positions.

Maplets [12] are a probabilistic hash index for LSM-trees. A Maplet maps a query key to a possible run in a level, eliminating the need to probe the filters at each run when performing queries. The Maplet approach is actually aligned with Disco. It avoids using filters and builds an index for a set of runs to map a key directly to a run. However, Maplets do not speed up range queries.

Much effort has been devoted to making B-trees and B+-trees more compact and versatile [3, 10, 34, 42]. The idea of extracting discriminative bits for a group of keys has been discussed in various occasions [5, 23, 27]. The Height Optimized Trie [6] maximizes the fan-out of an in-memory trie by extracting discriminative bits in various lengths to adaptively build an internal node. To the best of our knowledge, Disco is the first to apply discriminative bits to an LSM-tree design that enables I/O efficient queries.

**Improving reads on LSM-trees.** Nova-LSM [28] indexes runs only on the first level of an LSM-tree. Jungle [1] combines LSM-trees and copy-on-write B+-trees to improve the read performance at higher storage cost. Wisckey [35] separates the storage of keys from values to reduce the size of the LSM-tree and speed up reads. However, it introduces an extra layer of indirection and is not effective for small values. Bourbon [14] use learned models LSM-trees to accelerate reads, but it is limited to integer keys. Disco directly locates a result key for a query and supports variable-length string keys at a small storage cost.

**Reducing writes on B+-trees.** B$\epsilon$-tree [4, 8] allocates a buffer in each internal node to improve write performance on B+-tree. Compared to Disco, B$\epsilon$-tree still has mediocre read performance especially for range queries. In [41] the authors reduce the write amplification on B+-tree using special hardware that support transparent compression. Disco maintains efficient writes on commodity hardware.
sections.translationMarkdown:
有一类研究使用 filter 来减少 LSM-tree 上查询产生的 I/O。Filter 可以分为 point filter [7, 16, 18, 52] 和 range filter [31, 36, 47, 51]。如果 filter 认为目标 key 不存在于某个文件中，便可跳过读取该文件，从而提升查询性能。与 Disco 的索引方法相比，当 memory budget 有限时，filter 的有效性会下降。此外，filter 无法帮助定位 key。过滤之后，实际定位 key 仍需要额外的 I/O，而 Disco 可以直接精确定位目标 key 的位置。

Maplet [12] 是一种用于 LSM-tree 的概率型 hash index。Maplet 将 query key 映射到某一 level 中一个可能的 run，从而在执行查询时无需探测每个 run 上的 filter。Maplet 方法实际上与 Disco 的思路一致：它避免使用 filter，并为一组 runs 构建索引，将 key 直接映射到 run。不过，Maplet 无法加速 range query。

大量工作致力于让 B-tree 和 B+-tree 更加紧凑和通用 [3, 10, 34, 42]。从一组 key 中提取 discriminative bit 的思想已在多种场合得到讨论 [5, 23, 27]。Height Optimized Trie [6] 通过提取不同长度的 discriminative bit 来自适应地构建 internal node，从而最大化 in-memory trie 的 fan-out。据我们所知，Disco 是首个将 discriminative bit 应用于 LSM-tree 设计、并实现 I/O 高效查询的工作。

**改进 LSM-tree 上的读取。** Nova-LSM [28] 只索引 LSM-tree 第一层上的 runs。Jungle [1] 将 LSM-tree 与 copy-on-write B+-tree 相结合，以更高的存储成本换取读性能提升。Wisckey [35] 将 key 与 value 的存储分离，以减小 LSM-tree 的大小并加快读取。不过，它引入了额外的一层间接访问，而且对于较小的 value 效果不佳。Bourbon [14] 使用 learned model LSM-tree 加速读取，但仅限于 integer key。Disco 能够直接定位查询的结果 key，并以较小的存储成本支持变长 string key。

**减少 B+-tree 上的写入。** B$\epsilon$-tree [4, 8] 在每个 internal node 中分配一个 buffer，以提高 B+-tree 的写性能。与 Disco 相比，B$\epsilon$-tree 的读性能仍较为一般，尤其是在 range query 上。在 [41] 中，作者利用支持透明压缩的特殊硬件降低 B+-tree 的 write amplification。Disco 则能在通用硬件上保持高效写入。

sections.title: 6 CONCLUSION
sections.originalMarkdown: In this work, we presented Disco, a compact multi-run index for LSM-trees. Disco enables point queries and short range queries to locate keys using just 1–2 I/Os. Our experimental evaluation showed Disco reduces the I/O cost of queries by 51% of the state-of-the-art LSM-tree indexing techniques like REMIX. We integrated Disco into an LSM-tree database, DiscoDB, demonstrating throughput improvements of 150–220% across different workloads and memory budgets. Disco brings LSM-tree read performance much closer to B+-trees, while retaining fast writes. The techniques presented provide a promising direction for developing LSM-tree indexes that are efficient for both reads and writes.
sections.translationMarkdown: 在本工作中，我们提出了 Disco，一种用于 LSM-tree 的紧凑 multi-run index。Disco 使 point query 和短 range query 仅需 1–2 次 I/O 即可定位 key。实验评估表明，与 REMIX 等最先进的 LSM-tree 索引技术相比，Disco 将查询的 I/O cost 降低了 51%。我们将 Disco 集成到一个 LSM-tree 数据库 DiscoDB 中，并证明在不同工作负载和 memory budget 下，吞吐量可提高 150–220%。Disco 在保持快速写入的同时，使 LSM-tree 的读性能更加接近 B+-tree。本文提出的技术为开发同时具备高效读写能力的 LSM-tree 索引提供了一个富有前景的方向。
